mrs strickland want to know is:--“how do my husband bring such lovely lovely waler from your station know all his money affair and be certain he do not buy it what want to know is:--“how do woman like mrs bronckhorst come to marry man like bronckhorst and my conundrum be the most unanswerable of the three venus annodomini and the year go on as the year must do but our great diana be always new-- fresh and bloom and blonde and fair with azure eye and with aureate hair and all the folk as they come or go offer her praise to her heart 's content diana of ephesus she have nothing to do with number eighteen in the braccio nuovo of the vatican between visconti 's ceres and the god of the nile she be purely an indian deity an anglo indian deity that be to say and we call she the venus annodomini to distinguish she from other annodominis of the same everlasting order there be legend among the hill that she have once be young but no live man be prepared to come forward and say boldly that the legend be true man ride up to simla and stay and go away and make their name and do their life 's work and return again to find the venus annodomini exactly as they have leave she she be as immutable as the hill but not quite so green all that girl of eighteen could do in the way of rid walk dancing picnicking and over exertion generally the venus annodomini do and show no sign of fatigue or trace of weariness besides perpetual youth she have discover man say the secret of perpetual health and her fame spread about the land from mere woman she grow to be an institution insomuch that no young man could be say to be properly form who have not at some time or another worship at the shrine of the venus annodomini there be no one like she though there be many imitation six year in her eye be no more than six month to ordinary woman and ten make less visible impression on she than do week 's fever on an ordinary woman every one adore she and in return she be pleasant and courteous to nearly every one youth have be habit of hers for so long that she could not part with it never realize in fact the necessity of part with it and take for her more choose associate young people among the worshipper of the venus annodomini be young gayerson very young gayerson he be call to distinguish he from his father young gayerson bengal civilian who affect the custom as he have the heart of youth very young gayerson be not content to worship placidly and for form 's sake as the other young man do or to accept ride or dance or talk from the venus annodomini in properly humble and thankful spirit he be exact and therefore the venus annodomini repress he he worry himself nearly sick in futile sort of way over she and his devotion and earnestness make he appear either shy or boisterous or rude as his mood might vary by the side of the old man who with he bow before the venus annodomini she be sorry for he he remind she of lad who three and twenty year ago have profess boundless devotion for she and for whom in return she have feel something more than week 's weakness but that lad have fall away and marry another woman less than year after he have worship she and the venus annodomini have almost not quite forget his name very young gayerson have the same big blue eye and the same way of pout his underlip when he be excited or trouble
but the venus annodomini check he sternly none the less too much zeal be thing that she do not approve of prefer instead temper and sober tenderness very young gayerson be miserable and take no trouble to conceal his wretchedness he be in the army line regiment think but be not certain and since his face be look glass and his forehead an open book by reason of his innocence his brother in arm make his life burden to he and embitter his naturally sweet disposition no one except very young gayerson and he never tell his view know how old very young gayerson believe the venus annodomini to be perhaps he think she five and twenty or perhaps she tell he that she be this age very young gayerson would have ford the gugger in flood to carry her light word and have implicit faith in she every one like he and every one be sorry when they see he so bind slave of the venus annodomini every one too admit that it be not her fault for the venus annodomini differ from mrs hauksbee and mrs reiver in this particular she never move finger to attract any one but like ninon de l'enclos all man be attract to she one could admire and respect mrs hauksbee despise and avoid mrs reiver but one be force to adore the venus annodomini very young gayerson 's papa hold division or collectorate or something administrative in particularly unpleasant part of bengal full of babus who edit newspaper prove that young gayerson be nero and scylla and charybdis and in addition to the babus there be good deal of dysentery and cholera abroad for nine month of the year young gayerson he be about five and forty rather like babus they amuse he but he object to dysentery and when he could get away go to darjille for the most part this particular season he fancy that he would come up to simla and see his boy the boy be not altogether pleased he tell the venus annodomini that his father be come up and she flush little and say that she should be delighted to make his acquaintance then she look long and thoughtfully at very young gayerson because she be very very sorry for he and he be very very big idiot my daughter be come out in fortnight

young gayerson demmande that they should call on the venus annodomini very young gayerson go with his papa feeling somehow uncomfortable and small the venus annodomini receive they graciously and young gayerson said:--“by jove it be kitty very young gayerson would have listen for an explanation if his time have not be take up with try to talk to large handsome quiet well dress girl introduce to he by the venus annodomini as her daughter she be far old in manner style and repose than very young gayerson and as he realize this thing he feel sick presently he hear the venus annodomini saying:--“do you know that your son be one of my most devoted admirer do not wonder say young gayerson here he raise his voice:--“he follow his father 's footstep do not worship the ground you tread on ever so long ago kitty
and you have not change since then how strange it all seem very young gayerson say nothing his conversation with the daughter of the venus annodomini be through the rest of the call fragmentary and disjointed at five to morrow then say the venus annodomini and mind you be punctual at five punctual say young gayerson you can lend your old father horse dare say youngster can not you be go for ride tomorrow afternoon certainly say very young gayerson be go down to morrow morning my pony be at your service sir the venus annodomini look at he across the half light of the room and her big gray eye fill with moisture she rise and shake hand with he good
bye tom whisper the venus annodomini the bisara of pooree little blind fish thou art marvellous wise little blind fish who put out thy eye open thine ear while whisper my wish-- bring lover thou little blind fish the charm of the bisara
some native say that it come from the other side of kulu where the eleven inch temple sapphire be other that it be make at the devil shrine of ao chung in thibet be steal by kafir from he by gurkha from he again by lahouli from he by khitmatgar and by this latter sell to an englishman so all its virtue be lose because to work properly the bisara of pooree must be steal with bloodshed if possible but at any rate steal these story of the come into india be all false it be make at pooree age since the manner of its making would fill small book be steal by one of the temple dance girl there for her own purpose and then pass on from hand to hand steadily northward till it reach hanla always bear the same name the bisara of pooree in shape it be tiny square box of silver stud outside with eight small bala ruby inside the box which open with spring be little eyeless fish carve from some sort of dark shiny nut and wrap in shred of fade gold cloth that be the bisara of pooree and it be well for man to take king cobra in his hand than to touch the bisara of pooree all kind of magic be out of date and do away with except in india where nothing change in spite of the shiny toy scum stuff that people call civilization any man who know about the bisara of pooree will tell you what its power be always suppose that it have be honestly steal it be the only regularly work trustworthy love charm in the country with one exception the other charm be in the hand of trooper of the nizam 's horse at place call tuprani due north of hyderabad this can be depend upon for fact some one else may explain it if the bisara be not steal but give or buy or find it turn against its owner in three year and lead to ruin or death this be another fact which you may explain when you have time meanwhile you can laugh at it at present the bisara be safe on an ekka pony 's neck inside the blue bead necklace that keep off the evil eye if the ekka driver ever find it and wear it or give it to his wife be sorry for he very dirty hill cooly woman with goitre own it at theog in it come into simla from the north before churton 's khitmatgar buy it and sell it for three time its silver value to churton who collect curiosity the servant know no more what he have buy than the master but man look over churton 's collection of curiosity churton be an assistant commissioner by the way see and hold his tongue he be an englishman but know how to believe which show that he be different from most englishman he know that it be dangerous to have any share in the little box when work or dormant for unsought love be terrible gift pack--“grubby pack as we use to call he be in every way nasty little man who must have crawl into the army by mistake he be three inch tall than his sword but not half so strong and the sword be fifty shilling tailor make one nobody like he and suppose it be his wizenedness and worthlessness that make he fall so hopelessly in love with miss hollis who be good and sweet and five foot seven in her tennis shoe he be not content with fall in love quietly but bring all the strength of his miserable little nature into the business if he have not be so objectionable one might have pity he he vapore and fret and fume and trot up and down and try to make himself pleasing in miss hollis 's big quiet gray eye and fail it be one of the case that you sometimes meet even in this country where we marry by code of really blind attachment all on one side without the faint possibility of return miss hollis look on pack as some sort of vermin run about the road he have no prospect beyond captain 's pay and no wit to help that out by one anna in large sized man love like his would have be touch in good man it would have be grand he be what he be it be only nuisance you will believe this much what you will not believe be what follow churton and the man who know that the bisara be be lunch at the simla club together churton be complain of life in general his good mare have roll out of stable down the hill and have break she back his decision be be reverse by the upper court more than an assistant commissioner of eight year standing have right to expect he know liver and fever and for week past have feel out of sort altogether he be disgusted and dishearten simla club dining room be build as all the world know in two section with an arch arrangement divide they come in turn to your own left take the table under the window and you can not see any one who have come in turn to the right and take table on the right side of the arch curiously enough every word that you say can be hear not only by the other diner but by the servant beyond the screen through which they bring dinner this be worth know an echo room be trap to be forewarn against half in fun and half hop to be believe the man who know tell churton the story of the bisara of pooree at rather great length than have tell it to you in this place wind up with the suggestion that churton might as well throw the little box down the hill and see whether all his trouble would go with it in ordinary ear english ear the tale be only an interesting bit of folk lore churton laugh say that he feel well for his tiffin and go out pack have be tiffine by himself to the right of the arch and have hear everything he be nearly mad with his absurd infatuation for miss hollis that all simla have be laugh about it be curious thing that when man hat or love beyond reason he be ready to go beyond reason to gratify his feeling which he would not do for money or power merely depend upon it solomon would never have build altar to ashtaroth and all those lady with queer name if there have not be trouble of some kind in his zenana and nowhere else
but this be beside the story the fact of the case be these pack call on churton next day when churton be out leave his card and steal the bisara of pooree from its place under the clock on the mantelpiece steal it like the thief he be by nature three day later all simla be electrify by the news that miss hollis have accept pack the shrivelled rat pack do you desire clear evidence than this the bisara of pooree have be steal and it work as it have always do when win by foul mean there be three or four time in man 's life when he be justify in meddle with other people 's affair to play providence the man who know feel that he be justify but believe and act on belief be quite different thing the insolent satisfaction of pack as he amble by the side of miss hollis and churton 's striking release from liver as soon as the bisara of pooree have go decide the man he explain to churton and churton laugh because he be not bring up to believe that man on the government house list steal at least little thing but the miraculous acceptance by miss hollis of that tailor pack decide he to take step on suspicion he vow that he only want to find out where his ruby stud silver box have vanish to you can not accuse man on the government house list of steal and if you rifle his room you be thief yourself churton prompt by the man who know decide on burglary if he find nothing in pack 's room
but it be not nice to think of what would have happen in that case pack go to dance at benmore benmore be benmore in those day and not an office and dance fifteen waltz out of twenty two with miss hollis churton and the man take all the key that they could lay hand on and go to pack 's room in the hotel certain that his servant would be away pack be cheap soul he have not purchase decent cash box to keep his paper in but one of those native imitation that you buy for ten rupee it open to any sort of key and there at the bottom under pack 's insurance policy lie the bisara of pooree churton call pack name put the bisara of pooree in his pocket and go to the dance with the man at least he come in time for supper and see the beginning of the end in miss hollis 's eye she be hysterical after supper and be take away by her mamma at the dance with the abominable bisara in his pocket churton twist his foot on one of the step lead down to the old rink and have to be send home in rickshaw grumble he do not believe in the bisara of pooree any the more for this manifestation but he seek out pack and call he some ugly name and thief be the mild of they pack take the name with the nervous smile of little man who want both soul and body to resent an insult and go his way there be no public scandal week later pack get his definite dismissal from miss hollis there have be mistake in the placing of her affection she say
so he go away to madras where he can do no great harm even if he live to be colonel churton insist upon the man who know take the bisara of pooree as gift the man take it go down to the cart road at once find an ekka pony with blue head necklace fasten the bisara of pooree inside the necklace with piece of shoe string and thank heaven that he be rid of danger remember in case you ever find it that you must not destroy the bisara of pooree have not time to explain why just now
but the power lie in the little wooden fish mister gubernatis or max muller could tell you more about it than i. you will say that all this story be make up very well if ever you come across little silver ruby stud box seven eighth of an inch long by three quarter wide with dark brown wooden fish wrap in gold cloth inside it keep it keep it for three year
and then you will discover for yourself whether my story be true or false well still steal it as pack do
and you will be sorry that you have not kill yourself in the beginning the gate of hundred sorrow if can attain heaven for pice why should you be envious opium smoker 's proverb this be no work of mine my friend gabral misquitta the half caste speak it all between moonset and morning six week before he die and take it down from his mouth as he answer my question so:-- it lie between the copper smith 's gully and the pipe stem seller quarter within hundred yard too as the crow fly of the mosque of wazir khan do not mind tell any one this much but defy he to find the gate however well he may think he know the city you might even go through the very gully it stand in hundred time and be none the wise we use to call the gully the gully of the black smoke but its native name be altogether different of course load donkey could not pass between the wall and at one point just before you reach the gate bulge house front make people go along all sideways it be not really gate though it be house old fung tching have it first five year ago he be boot maker in calcutta they say that he murder his wife there when he be drunk that be why he drop bazar rum and take to the black smoke instead later on he come up north and open the gate as house where you could get your smoke in peace and quiet mind you
it be pukka respectable opium house and not one of those stifle sweltering chandoo khana that you can find all over the city no the old man know his business thoroughly and he be most clean for chinaman he be one eyed little chap not much more than five foot high and both his middle finger be go all the same he be the handy man at roll black pill have ever see never seem to be touch by the smoke either and what he take day and night night and day be caution have be at it five year and can do my fair share of the smoke with any one but be child to fung tche that way all the same the old man be keen on his money very keen and that be what can not understand hear he save good deal before he die but his nephew have get all that now and the old man be go back to china to be bury he keep the big upper room where his good customer gather as neat as new pin in one corner use to stand fung tching 's joss almost as ugly as fung tching and there be always stick burn under his nose
but you never smell they when the pipe be go thick opposite the joss be fung tching 's coffin he have spend good deal of his saving on that and whenever new man come to the gate he be always introduce to it it be lacquer black with red and gold writing on it and have hear that fung tche bring it out all the way from china do not know whether that be true or not but know that if come first in the evening use to spread my mat just at the foot of it it be quiet corner you see and sort of breeze from the gully come in at the window now and then besides the mat there be no other furniture in the room only the coffin and the old joss all green and blue and purple with age and polish fung tching never tell we why he call the place the gate of hundred sorrow he be the only chinaman know who use bad sound fancy name most of they be flowery as you will see in calcutta we use to find that out for ourselves nothing grow on you so much if you be white as the black smoke yellow man be make different opium do not tell on he scarcely at all but white and black suffer good deal of course there be some people that the smoke do not touch any more than tobacco would at first they just doze bit as one would fall asleep naturally and next morning they be almost fit for work now be one of that sort when begin but have be at it for five year pretty steadily and its different now there be an old aunt of mine down agra way
and she leave little at her death about sixty rupee month secure sixty be not much can recollect time seem hundred and hundred of year ago that be get my three hundred month and pick when be work on big timber contract in calcutta do not stick to that work for long the black smoke do not allow of much other business and even though be very little affect by it as man go could not do day 's work now to save my life after all sixty rupee be what want when old fung tching be alive he use to draw the money for give about half of it to live on eat very little and the rest he keep himself be free of the gate at any time of the day and night and could smoke and sleep there when like so do not care know the old man make good thing out of it but that be no matter nothing matter much to and besides the money always come fresh and fresh each month there be ten of we meet at the gate when the place be first open and two baboos from government office somewhere in anarkulli
but they get the sack and could not pay no man who have to work in the daylight can do the black smoke for any length of time straight on chinaman that be fung tching 's nephew bazar woman that have get lot of money somehow an english loafer mac
somebody think but have forget that smoke heap but never seem to pay anything they say he have save fung tching 's life at some trial in calcutta when he be barrister another eurasian like myself from madras half caste woman and couple of man who say they have come from the north think they must have be persians or afghans or something there be not more than five of we live now
but we come regular do not know what happen to the baboos but the bazar woman she die after six month of the gate and think fung tching take her bangle and nose ring for himself but be not certain the englishman he drink as well as smoke
and he drop off one of the persians get kill in row at night by the big well near the mosque long time ago and the police shut up the well because they say it be full of foul air they find he dead at the bottom of it
so you see there be only the chinaman the half caste woman that we call the memsahib she use to live with fung tche the other eurasian and one of the persians the memsahib look very old now think she be young woman when the gate be open
but we be all old for the matter of that hundred and hundred of year old it be very hard to keep count of time in the gate and besides time do not matter to draw my sixty rupee fresh and fresh every month very very long while ago when use to be get three hundred and fifty rupee month and pick on big timber contract at calcutta have wife of sort
but she be dead now people say that kill she by take to the black smoke perhaps do but it be so long since it do not matter sometimes when first come to the gate use to feel sorry for it but that be all over and do with long ago and draw my sixty rupee fresh and fresh every month and be quite happy not drunk happy you know but always quiet and soothe and content how do take to it it begin at calcutta use to try it in my own house just to see what it be like never go very far but think my wife must have die then anyhow find myself here and get to know fung tching do not remember rightly how that come about but he tell of the gate and use to go there and somehow have never get away from it since mind you though the gate be respectable place in fung tching 's time where you could be comfortable and not at all like the chandoo khana where the nigger go no
it be clean and quiet and not crowd of course there be other beside we ten and the man
but we always have mat apiece with wadded woollen head piece all cover with black and red dragon and thing just like coffin in the corner at the end of one 's third pipe the dragon use to move about and fight have watch they many and many night through use to regulate my smoke that way
and now it take dozen pipe to make they stir besides they be all tear and dirty like the mat and old fung tching be dead he die couple of year ago and give the pipe always use now silver one with queer beast crawl up and down the receiver bottle below the cup before that think use big bamboo stem with copper cup very small one and green jade mouthpiece it be little thick than walk stick stem and smoke sweet very sweet the bamboo seem to suck up the smoke silver
do not and have get to clean it out now and then that be great deal of trouble but smoke it for the old man 's sake he must have make good thing out of but he always give clean mat and pillow and the good stuff you could get anywhere when he die his nephew tsin ling take up the gate and he call it the temple of the three possession but we old one speak of it as the hundred sorrow all the same the nephew do thing very shabbily and think the memsahib must help he she live with he same as she use to do with the old man the two let in all sort of low people nigger and all and the black smoke be not as good as it use to be have find burn bran in my pipe over and over again the old man would have die if that have happen in his time besides the room be never clean and all the mat be tear and cut at the edge the coffin have go go to china again with the old man and two ounce of smoke inside it in case he should want they on the way the joss do not get so many stick burn under his nose as he use to that be sign of ill luck as sure as death he be all brown too and no one ever attend to he that be the memsahib 's work know because when tsin ling try to burn gilt paper before he she say it be waste of money and if he keep stick burn very slowly the joss would not know the difference so now we have get the stick mix with lot of glue and they take half an hour long to burn and smell stinky let alone the smell of the room by itself no business can get on if they try that sort of thing the joss do not like it can see that late at night sometimes he turn all sort of queer color blue and green and red just as he use to do when old fung tching be alive and he roll his eye and stamp his foot like devil do not know why do not leave the place and smoke quietly in little room of my own in the bazar most like tsin ling would kill if go away he draw my sixty rupee now and besides it be so much trouble and have grow to be very fond of the gate it be not much to look at not what it be in the old man 's time but could not leave it have see so many come in and out and have see so many die here on the mat that should be afraid of die in the open now have see some thing that people would call strange enough but nothing be strange when you be on the black smoke except the black smoke and if it be it would not matter fung tching use to be very particular about his people and never get in any one who would give trouble by die messy and such but the nephew be not half so careful he tell everywhere that he keep first chop house never try to get man in quietly and make they comfortable like fung tching do that be why the gate be get little bit more known than it use to be among the nigger of course the nephew dare not get white or for matter of that mixed skin into the place he have to keep we three of course and the memsahib and the other eurasian we be fixture
but he would not give we credit for pipeful not for anything one of these day hope shall die in the gate the persian and the madras man be terrible shaky now they have get boy to light their pipe for they always do that myself most like shall see they carry out before do not think shall ever outlive the memsahib or tsin ling woman last long than man at the black smoke and tsin ling have deal of the old man 's blood in he though he do smoke cheap stuff the bazar woman know when she be go two day before her time
and she die on clean mat with nicely wad pillow and the old man hang up her pipe just above the joss he be always fond of she fancy
but he take her bangle just the same should like to die like the bazar woman on clean cool mat with pipe of good stuff between my lip when feel be go shall ask tsin ling for they and he can draw my sixty rupee month fresh and fresh as long as he please and watch the black and red dragon have their last big fight together and
then well it do not matter nothing matter much to only wish tsin ling would not put bran into the black smoke the story of muhammad din who be the happy man he that see in his own house at home little child crown with dust leap and fall and cry munichandra translate by professor peterson the polo ball be an old one scarred chip and dinte it stand on the mantelpiece among the pipe stem which imam din khitmatgar be clean for do the heaven bear want this ball say imam din deferentially the heaven bear set no particular store by it but of what use be polo ball to khitmatgar by your honor 's favor have little son he have see this ball and desire it to play with do not want it for myself no one would for an instant accuse portly old imam din of want to play with polo ball he carry out the battered thing into the verandah and there follow hurricane of joyful squeak patter of small foot and the thud thud thud of the ball roll along the ground evidently the little son have be wait outside the door to secure his treasure but how have he manage to see that polo ball next day come back from office half an hour early than usual be aware of small figure in the dining room tiny plump figure in ridiculously inadequate shirt which come perhaps half way down the tubby stomach it wander round the room thumb in mouth croon to itself as it take stock of the picture undoubtedly this be the little son he have no business in my room of course but be so deeply absorb in his discovery that he never notice in the doorway step into the room and startle he nearly into fit he sit down on the ground with gasp his eye open and his mouth follow suit know what be come and flee follow by long dry howl which reach the servant quarter far more quickly than any command of mine have ever do in ten second imam din be in the dining room then despairing sob arise and return to find imam din admonish the small sinner who be use most of his shirt as handkerchief this boy say imam din judicially be budmash big budmash he will without doubt go to the jail khana for his behavior renew yell from the penitent and an elaborate apology to myself from imam din tell the baby say that the sahib be not angry and take he away imam din convey my forgiveness to the offender who have now gather all his shirt round his neck string wise and the yell subside into sob the two set off for the door his name say imam din as though the name be part of the crime be muhammad din and he be budmash free from present danger muhammad din turn round in his father 's arm and say gravely:--“it be true that my name be muhammad din tahib but be not budmash be man from that day date my acquaintance with muhammad din never again do he come into my dining room but on the neutral ground of the compound we greet each other with much state though our conversation be confine to talaam tahib from his side and salaam muhammad din from mine daily on my return from office the little white shirt and the fat little body use to rise from the shade of the creeper cover trellis where they have be hide and daily check my horse here that my salutation might not be slur over or give unseemly muhammad din never have any companion he use to trot about the compound in and out of the castor oil bush on mysterious errand of his own one day stumble upon some of his handiwork far down the ground he have half bury the polo ball in dust and stick six shrivelled old marigold flower in circle round it outside that circle again be rude square trace out in bit of red brick alternate with fragment of break china the whole bind by little bank of dust the bhistie from the well curb put in plea for the small architect say that it be only the play of baby and do not much disfigure my garden heaven know that have no intention of touch the child 's work then or later but that evening stroll through the garden bring unawares full on it so that trample before know marigold head dust bank and fragment of broken soap dish into confusion past all hope of mend next morning come upon muhammad din cry softly to himself over the ruin have work some one have cruelly tell he that the sahib be very angry with he for spoil the garden and have scatter his rubbish use bad language the while muhammad din labor for an hour at efface every trace of the dust bank and pottery fragment and it be with tearful apologetic face that he say talaam tahib when come home from the office hasty inquiry result in imam din inform muhammad din that by my singular favor he be permit to disport himself as he please whereat the child take heart and fall to trace the ground plan of an edifice which be to eclipse the marigold polo ball creation for some month the chubby little eccentricity revolve in his humble orbit among the castor oil bush and in the dust always fashion magnificent palace from stale flower throw away by the bearer smooth water wear pebble bit of broken glass and feather pull fancy from my fowl always alone and always croon to himself gayly spot sea shell be drop one day close to the last of his little building and look that muhammad din should build something more than ordinarily splendid on the strength of it nor be disappoint he meditate for the well part of an hour and his croon rise to jubilant song then he begin trace in dust it would certainly be wondrous palace this one for it be two yard long and yard broad in ground plan but the palace be never complete next day there be no muhammad din at the head of the carriage drive and no talaam tahib to welcome my return have grow accustomed to the greeting and its omission trouble next day imam din tell that the child be suffer slightly from fever and need quinine he get the medicine and an english doctor they have no stamina these brat say the doctor as he leave imam din 's quarter week later though would have give much to have avoid it meet on the road to the mussulman bury ground imam din accompany by one other friend carry in his arm wrap in white cloth
all that be leave of little muhammad din on the strength of likeness
if your mirror be break look into still water but have care that you do not fall in hindu proverb next to requite attachment one of the most convenient thing that young man can carry about with he at the beginning of his career be an unrequited attachment it make he feel important and business like and blase and cynical and whenever he have touch of liver or suffer from want of exercise he can mourn over his lost love and be very happy in tender twilight fashion hannasyde 's affair of the heart have be godsend to he it be four year old and the girl have long since give up thinking of it she have marry and have many care of her own in the beginning she have tell hannasyde that while she could never be anything more than sister to he she would always take the deep interest in his welfare this startlingly new and original remark give hannasyde something to think over for two year and his own vanity fill in the other twenty four month hannasyde be quite different from phil garron but none the less have several point in common with that far too lucky man he keep his unrequited attachment by he as man keep well smoke pipe for comfort 's sake and because it have grow dear in the use it bring he happily through the simla season hannasyde be not lovely there be crudity in his manner and roughness in the way in which he help lady on to her horse that do not attract the other sex to he even if he have cast about for their favor which he do not he keep his wound heart all to himself for while then trouble come to he all who go to simla know the slope from the telegraph to the public work office hannasyde be loaf up the hill one september morning between call hour when rickshaw come down in hurry and in the rickshaw sit the live breathing image of the girl who have make he so happily unhappy hannasyde lean against the railing and gasp he want to run downhill after the rickshaw but that be impossible so he go forward with most of his blood in his temple it be impossible for many reason that the woman in the rickshaw could be the girl he have know she be he discover later the wife of man from dindigul or coimbatore or some out of the way place and she have come up to simla early in the season for the good of her health she be go back to dindigul or wherever it be at the end of the season and in all likelihood would never return to simla again her proper hill station be ootacamund that night hannasyde raw and savage from the rake up of all old feeling take counsel with himself for one measure hour what he decide upon be this and you must decide for yourself how much genuine affection for the old love and how much very natural inclination to go abroad and enjoy himself affect the decision mrs landys haggert would never in all human likelihood cross his path again
so whatever he do do not much matter she be marvellously like the girl who take deep interest and the rest of the formula all thing consider it would be pleasant to make the acquaintance of mrs landys haggert and for little time only very little time to make believe that he be with alice chisane again every one be more or less mad on one point hannasyde 's particular monomania be his old love alice chisane he make it his business to get introduce to mrs haggert and the introduction prosper he also make it his business to see as much as he could of that lady when man be in earnest as to interview the facility which simla offer be startling there be garden party and tennis party and picnic and luncheon at annandale and rifle match and dinner and ball besides ride and walk which be matter of private arrangement hannasyde have start with the intention of see likeness and he end by do much more he want to be deceive he mean to be deceive and he deceive himself very thoroughly not only be the face and figure the face and figure of alice chisane but the voice and low tone be exactly the same and so be the turn of speech and the little mannerism that every woman have of gait and gesticulation be absolutely and identically the same the turn of the head be the same the tired look in the eye at the end of long walk be the same the sloop and wrench over the saddle to hold in pull horse be the same and once most marvellous of all mrs landys haggert sing to herself in the next room while hannasyde be wait to take she for ride hum note for note with throaty quiver of the voice in the second line:--“poor wander one exactly as alice chisane have hum it for hannasyde in the dusk of an english drawing room in the actual woman herself in the soul of she there be not the least likeness she and alice chisane be cast in different mould but all that hannasyde want to know and see and think about be this maddening and perplexing likeness of face and voice and manner he be bent on make fool of himself that way and he be in no sort disappoint open and obvious devotion from any sort of man be always pleasant to any sort of woman but mrs landys haggert be woman of the world could make nothing of hannasyde 's admiration he would take any amount of trouble he be selfish man habitually to meet and forestall if possible she wish anything she tell he to do be law and he be there could be no doubt it fond of her company so long as she talk to he and keep on talk about triviality but when she launch into expression of her personal view and her wrong those small social difference that make the spice of simla life hannasyde be neither pleased nor interested he do not want to know anything about mrs landys haggert or her experience in the past she have travel nearly all over the world and could talk cleverly he want the likeness of alice chisane before his eye and her voice in his ear anything outside that remind he of another personality jar and he show that it do under the new post office one evening mrs landys haggert turn on he and speak her mind shortly and without warn mr hannasyde say she will you be good enough to explain why you have appoint yourself my special cavalier servente do not understand it but be perfectly certain somehow or other that you do not care the least little bit in the world for this seem to support by the way the theory that no man can act or tell lie to woman without be find out hannasyde be take off his guard his defence never be strong one because he be always think of himself and he blurt out before he know what he be say this inexpedient answer:--“no more do the queerness of the situation and the reply make mrs landys haggert laugh then it all come out and at the end of hannasyde 's lucid explanation mrs haggert say with the least little touch of scorn in her voice:--“so be to act as the lay figure for you to hang the rag of your tattered affection on be hannasyde do not see what answer be require and he devote himself generally and vaguely to the praise of alice chisane which be unsatisfactory now it be to be thoroughly make clear that mrs haggert have not the shadow of ghost of an interest in hannasyde only only no woman like be make love through instead of to specially on behalf of musty divinity of four year stand hannasyde do not see that he have make any very particular exhibition of himself he be glad to find sympathetic soul in the arid waste of simla when the season end hannasyde go down to his own place and mrs haggert to hers it be like make love to ghost say hannasyde to himself and it do not matter and now will get to my work
but he find himself think steadily of the haggert chisane ghost and he could not be certain whether it be haggert or chisane that make up the great part of the pretty phantom he get understand month later peculiar point of this peculiar country be the way in which heartless government transfer man from one end of the empire to the other you can never be sure of getting rid of friend or an enemy till he or she die there be case once but that be another story haggert 's department order he up from dindigul to the frontier at two day notice and he go through lose money at every step from dindigul to his station he drop mrs haggert at lucknow to stay with some friend there to take part in big ball at the chutter munzil and to come on when he have make the new home little comfortable lucknow be hannasyde 's station and mrs haggert
stay week there hannasyde go to meet she and the train come in he discover which he have be think of for the past month the unwisdom of his conduct also strike he the lucknow week with two dance and an unlimited quantity of ride together clinch matter and hannasyde find himself pace this circle of thought:--he adore alice chisane at least he have adore she and he admire mrs landys haggert because she be like alice chisane
but mrs landys haggert be not in the least like alice chisane be thousand time more adorable now alice chisane be the bride of another and so be mrs landys haggert and good and honest wife
too therefore he hannasyde be here he call himself several hard name and wish that he have be wise in the beginning whether mrs landys haggert see what be go on in his mind she alone know he seem to take an unqualified interest in everything connect with herself as distinguish from the alice chisane likeness and he say one or two thing which if alice chisane have be still betroth to he could scarcely have be excuse even on the ground of the likeness
but mrs haggert turn the remark aside and spend long time in make hannasyde see what comfort and pleasure she have be to he because of her strange resemblance to his old love hannasyde groan in his saddle and say yes indeed and busy himself with preparation for her departure to the frontier feel very small and miserable the last day of she stay at lucknow come and hannasyde see she off at the railway station she be very grateful for his kindness and the trouble he have take and smile pleasantly and sympathetically as one who know the alice chisane reason of that kindness and hannasyde abuse the cooly with the luggage and hustle the people on the platform and pray that the roof might fall in and slay he as the train go out slowly mrs landys haggert lean out of the window to say goodbye:--“on second think au revoir mr hannasyde go home in the spring and perhaps may meet you in town hannasyde shake hand and say very earnestly and adoringly:--“i hope to heaven shall never see your face again and mrs haggert understand wressley of the foreign office close and draw for my love 's sake that now be false to and slew the riever of tarrant moss and set dumeny free and ever they give praise and gold and ever moan my loss for strike the blow for my false love 's sake and not for the man at the moss tarrant moss one of the many curse of our life out here be the want of atmosphere in the painter 's sense there be no half tint worth notice man stand out all crude and raw with nothing to tone they down and nothing to scale they against they do their work and grow to think that there be nothing but their work and nothing like their work and that they be the real pivot on which the administration turn here be an instance of this feeling half caste clerk be rule form in pay office he say to me:--“do
you know what would happen if add or take away one single line on this sheet then with the air of conspirator:--“it would disorganize the whole of the treasury payment throughout the whole of the presidency circle think of that if man have not this delusion as to the ultra importance of their own particular employment suppose that they would sit down and kill themselves but their weakness be wearisome particularly when the listener know that he himself commit exactly the same sin even the secretariat believe that it do good when it ask an over drive executive officer to take census of wheat weevil through district of five thousand square mile there be man once in the foreign office man who have grow middle age in the department and be commonly say by irreverent junior to be able to repeat aitchison 's treaty and sunnud backwards in his sleep what he do with his store knowledge only the secretary know
and he naturally would not publish the news abroad this man 's name be wressley
and it be the shibboleth in those day to say:--“wressley know more about the central indian state than any live man if you do not say this you be consider one of mean understanding now day the man who say that he know the ravel of the inter tribal complication across the border be of more use but in wressley 's time much attention be pay to the central indian state they be call focus and factor and all manner of impose name and here the curse of anglo indian life fall heavily when wressley lift up his voice and speak about such and such succession to such and such throne the foreign office be silent and head of department repeat the last two or three word of wressley 's sentence and tack
yes yes on they and know that they be assist the empire to grapple with serious political contingency in most big undertaking one or two man do the work while the rest sit near and talk till the ripe decoration begin to fall wressley be the work member of the foreign office firm and to keep he up to his duty when he show sign of flag he be make much of by his superior and tell what fine fellow he be he do not require coax because he be of tough build but what he receive confirm he in the belief that there be no one quite so absolutely and imperatively necessary to the stability of india as wressley of the foreign office there might be other good man but the know honor and trusted man among man be wressley of the foreign office we have viceroy in those day who know exactly when to gentle fractious big man and to hearten up collar gall little one and so keep all his team level he convey to wressley the impression which have just set down and even tough man be apt to be disorganized by viceroy 's praise there be case once but that be another story all india know wressley 's name and office it be in thacker and spink 's directory but who he be personally or what he do or what his special merit be not fifty man know or care his work fill all his time
and he find no leisure to cultivate acquaintance beyond those of dead rajput chief with ahir blot in their scutcheon wressley would have make very good clerk in the herald 's college have he not be bengal civilian upon day between office and office great trouble come to wressley overwhelm he knock he down and leave he gasp as though he have be little school boy without reason against prudence and at moment 's notice he fall in love with frivolous golden haired girl who use to tear about simla mall on high rough waler with blue velvet jockey cap cram over her eye her name be venner tillie venner
and she be delightful she take wressley 's heart at hand gallop and wressley find that it be not good for man to live alone even with half the foreign office record in his press then simla laugh for wressley in love be slightly ridiculous he do his good to interest the girl in himself that be to say his work and she after the manner of woman do she good to appear interested in what behind his back she call mr wressley 's wajah for she lisp very prettily she do not understand one little thing about they but she act as if she do man have marry on that sort of error before now providence however have care of wressley he be immensely strike with miss venner 's intelligence he would have be more impressed have he hear her private and confidential account of his call he hold peculiar notion as to the wooing of girl he say that the good work of man 's career should be lie reverently at their foot ruskin write something like this somewhere think but in ordinary life few kiss be well and save time about month after he have lose his heart to miss venner and have be do his work vilely in consequence the first idea of his native rule in central india strike wressley and fill he with joy it be as he sketch it great thing the work of his life really comprehensive survey of most fascinating subject to be write with all the special and laboriously acquire knowledge of wressley of the foreign office gift fit for an empress he tell miss venner that he be go to take leave and hop on his return to bring she present worthy of her acceptance would she wait certainly she would wressley draw seventeen hundred rupee month she would wait year for that her mamma would help she to wait so wressley take one year 's leave and all the available document about truck load that he could lay hand on and go down to central india with his notion hot in his head he begin his book in the land he be write of too much official correspondence have make he frigid workman
and he must have guess that he need the white light of local color on his palette this be dangerous paint for amateur to play with heaven how that man work he catch his rajah analyze his rajah and trace they up into the mist of time and beyond with their queen and their concubine he date and cross date pedigreed and triple pedigreed compare note connote wove string sort select infer calendar and counter calendar for ten hour day and because this sudden and new light of love be upon he he turn those dry bone of history and dirty record of misdeed into thing to weep or to laugh over as he please his heart and soul be at the end of his pen
and they get into the link he be dower with sympathy insight humor and style for two hundred and thirty day and night and his book be book he have his vast special knowledge with he so to speak but the spirit the weave in human touch the poetry and the power of the output be beyond all special knowledge but doubt whether he know the gift that be in he then and thus he may have lose some happiness he be toil for tillie venner not for himself man often do their good work blind for some one else 's sake also though this have nothing to do with the story in india where every one know every one else you can watch man be drive by the woman who govern they out of the rank and file and send to take up point alone good man once start go forward but an average man so soon as the woman lose interest in his success as tribute to her power come back to the battalion and be no more hear of wressley bear the first copy of his book to simla and blush and stammering present it to miss venner she read little of it give her review verbatim:--“oh your book it be all about those how wid wajah do not understand it wressley of the foreign office be break smashed,--i be not exaggerate by this one frivolous little girl all that he could say feebly was:--“but
but it be my magnum opus the work of my life miss venner do not know what magnum opus mean
but she know that captain kerrington have win three race at the last gymkhana wressley do not press she to wait for he any long he have sense enough for that then come the reaction after the year 's strain and wressley go back to the foreign office and his wajah compile gazetteere report writing hack who would have be dear at three hundred rupee month he abide by miss venner 's review which prove that the inspiration in the book be purely temporary and unconnected with himself nevertheless he have no right to sink in hill tarn five packing case bring up at enormous expense from bombay of the good book of indian history ever write when he sell off before retire some year later be turn over his shelf and come across the only exist copy of native rule in central india”--the copy that miss venner could not understand read it sit on his mule truck as long as the light last and offer he his own price for it he look over my shoulder for few page and say to himself drearily:--“now how in the world do come to write such damn good stuff as that then to me:--“take it and keep it write one of your penny farthing yarn about its birth perhaps perhaps the whole business may have be ordain to that end which know what wressley of the foreign office be once strike as about the bitter thing that have ever hear man say of his own work by word of mouth not though you die to night sweet and wail spectre at my door shall mortal fear make love immortal fail-- shall but love you more who from death 's house return give still one moment 's comfort in my matchless ill shadow house this tale may be explain by those who know how soul be make and where the bound of the possible be put down have live long enough in this country to know that it be good to know nothing and can only write the story as it happen dumoise be our civil surgeon at meridki
and we call he dormouse because he be round little sleepy little man he be good doctor and never quarrel with any one not even with our deputy commissioner who have the manner of bargee and the tact of horse he marry girl as round and as sleepy look as himself she be miss hillardyce daughter of squash hillardyce of the berar who marry his chief 's daughter by mistake
but that be another story honeymoon in india be seldom more than week long
but there be nothing to hinder couple from extend it over two or three year this be delightful country for married folk who be wrap up in one another they can live absolutely alone and without interruption just as the dormice do these two little people retire from the world after their marriage and be very happy they be force of course to give occasional dinner
but they make no friend hereby and the station go its own way and forget they only say occasionally that dormouse be the good of good fellow though dull civil surgeon who never quarrel be rarity appreciate as such few people can afford to play robinson crusoe anywhere least of all in india where we be few in the land and very much dependent on each other 's kind office dumoise be wrong in shut himself from the world for year
and he discover his mistake when an epidemic of typhoid break out in the station in the heart of the cold weather and his wife go down he be shy little man and five day be waste before he realize that mrs dumoise be burn with something bad than simple fever and three day more pass before he venture to call on mrs shute the engineer 's wife and timidly speak about his trouble nearly every household in india know that doctor be very helpless in typhoid the battle must be fight out between death and the nurse minute by minute and degree by degree mrs shute almost box dumoise 's ear for what she call his criminal delay and go off at once to look after the poor girl we have seven case of typhoid in the station that winter and as the average of death be about one in every five case we feel certain that we should have to lose somebody but all do their good the woman sit up nurse the woman and the man turn to and tend the bachelor who be down and we wrestle with those typhoid case for fifty six day and bring they through the valley of the shadow in triumph
but just when we think all be over and be go to give dance to celebrate the victory little mrs dumoise get relapse and die in week and the station go to the funeral dumoise break down utterly at the brink of the grave and have to be take away after the death dumoise creep into his own house and refuse to be comfort he do his duty perfectly
but we all feel that he should go on leave and the other man of his own service tell he so dumoise be very thankful for the suggestion he be thankful for anything in those day and go to chini on walk tour chini be some twenty march from simla in the heart of the hill and the scenery be good if you be in trouble you pass through big still deodar forest and under big still cliff and over big still grass down swell like woman 's breast and the wind across the grass and the rain among the deodar says:--“hush hush hush so little dumoise be pack off to chini to wear down his grief with full plate camera and rifle he take also useless bearer because the man have be his wife 's favorite servant he be idle and thief but dumoise trust everything to he on his way back from chini dumoise turn aside to bagi through the forest reserve which be on the spur of mount huttoo some man who have travel more than little say that the march from kotegarh to bagi be one of the fine in creation it run through dark wet forest and end suddenly in bleak nip hill side and black rock bagi dak bungalow be open to all the wind and be bitterly cold few people go to bagi perhaps that be the reason why dumoise go there he halt at seven in the evening and his bearer go down the hill side to the village to engage cooly for the next day 's march the sun have set and the night wind be begin to croon among the rock dumoise lean on the railing of the verandah wait for his bearer to return the man come back almost immediately after he have disappear and at such rate that dumoise fancy he must have cross bear he be run as hard as he could up the face of the hill
but there be no bear to account for his terror he race to the verandah and fall down the blood spurt from his nose and his face iron gray then he gurgled:--“i have see the memsahib have see the memsahib where say dumoise down there walk on the road to the village she be in blue dress
and she lift the veil of her bonnet and said:--'ram dass give my salaam to the sahib and tell he that shall meet he next month at nuddea then run away because be afraid what dumoise say or do do not know ram dass declare that he say nothing but walk up and down the verandah all the cold night wait for the memsahib to come up the hill and stretch out his arm into the dark like madman
but no memsahib come and next day he go on to simla cross question the bearer every hour ram dass could only say that he have meet mrs dumoise and that she have lift up her veil and give he the message which he have faithfully repeat to dumoise to this statement ram dass adhere he do not know where nuddea be have no friend at nuddea and would most certainly never go to nuddea even though his pay be double nuddea be in bengal and have nothing whatever to do with doctor serve in the punjab it must be more than twelve hundred mile from meridki dumoise go through simla without halt and return to meridki there to take over charge from the man who have be officiate for he during his tour there be some dispensary account to be explain and some recent order of the surgeon general to be note and altogether the taking over be full day 's work in the evening dumoise tell his locum tenens who be an old friend of his bachelor day what have happen at bagi and the man say that ram dass might as well have choose tuticorin while he be about it at that moment telegraph peon come in with telegram from simla order dumoise not to take over charge at meridki but to go at once to nuddea on special duty there be nasty outbreak of cholera at nuddea and the bengal government be shorthande as usual have borrow surgeon from the punjab dumoise throw the telegram across the table and said:--“well the other doctor say nothing it be all that he could say then he remember that dumoise have pas through simla on his way from bagi and thus might possibly have hear the first news of the impending transfer he try to put the question and the imply suspicion into word but dumoise stop he with:--“if have desire that should never have come back from chini be shoot there wish to live for have thing to do but shall not be sorry the other man bow his head and help in the twilight to pack up dumoise 's just open trunk ram dass enter with the lamp where be the sahib go he ask to nuddea say dumoise softly ram dass claw dumoise 's knee and boot and beg he not to go ram dass weep and howl till he be turn out of the room then he wrap up all his belonging and come back to ask for character he be not go to nuddea to see his sahib die and perhaps to die himself so dumoise give the man his wage and go down to nuddea alone the other doctor bid he good
bye as one under sentence of death eleven day later he have join his memsahib and the bengal government have to borrow fresh doctor to cope with that epidemic at nuddea the first importation lie dead in chooadanga dak bungalow to be hold for reference by the hoof of the wild goat up toss from the cliff where she lie in the sun fall the stone to the tarn where the daylight be lose
so she fall from the light of the sun and alone now the fall be ordain from the first with the goat and the cliff and the tarn but the stone know only her life be accurse as she sink in the depth of the tarn and alone
oh thou who have build the world
oh thou who hast light the sun
oh thou who hast darken the tarn judge thou the sin of the stone that be hurl by the goat from the light of the sun as she sink in the mire of the tarn even now even now even now from the unpublished paper of mcintosh jellaludin say be it dawn be it dusk in thy bower thou whom long for who long for oh be it night be it-- here he fall over little camel colt that be sleep in the serai where the horse trader and the good of the blackguard from central asia live and because he be very drunk indeed and the night be dark he could not rise again till help he that be the beginning of my acquaintance with mcintosh jellaludin when loafer and drunk sing the song of the bower he must be worth cultivate he get off the camel 's back
and say rather thickly:--“i
be bit screw but dip in loggerhead will put right again and say have you speak to symond about the mare 's knee now loggerhead be six thousand weary mile away from we close to mesopotamia where you must not fish and poaching be impossible and charley symonds stable half mile far across the paddock it be strange to hear all the old name on may night among the horse and camel of the sultan caravanserai then the man seem to remember himself and sober down at the same time he lean against the camel and point to corner of the serai where lamp be burning:-- live there say he and should be extremely oblige if you would be good enough to help my mutinous foot thither for be more than usually drunk most most phenomenally tight but not in respect to my head my brain cry out
against'--how do it go
but my head rid on the roll on the dung hill should have say and control the qualm help he through the gang of tethered horse and he collapse on the edge of the verandah in front of the line of native quarter
thank thousand thank moon and little little star to think that man should so shamelessly infamous liquor too ovid in exile drink no bad it be freeze have no ice good night would introduce you to my wife be sober or she civilized native woman come out of the darkness of the room and begin call the man name so go away he be the most interesting loafer that have the pleasure of know for long time and later on he become friend of mine he be tall well build fair man fearfully shake with drink and he look near fifty than the thirty five which he say be his real age when man begin to sink in india and be not send home by his friend as soon as may be he fall very low from respectable point of view by the time that he change his creed as do mcintosh
he be past redemption in most big city native will tell you of two or three sahib generally low caste who have turn hindu or mussulman and who live more or less as such but it be not often that you can get to know they as mcintosh himself use to say:--“if change my religion for my stomach 's sake do not seek to become martyr to missionary nor be anxious for notoriety at the outset of acquaintance mcintosh warn remember this be not an object for charity require neither your money your food nor your cast off raiment be that rare animal self support drunkard if you choose will smoke with you for the tobacco of the bazar do not admit suit my palate and will borrow any book which you may not specially value it be more than likely that shall sell they for bottle of excessively filthy country liquor in return you shall share such hospitality as my house afford here be charpoy on which two can sit and it be possible that there may from time to time be food in that platter drink unfortunately you will find on the premise at any hour and thus make you welcome to all my poor establishment be admit to the mcintosh household and my good tobacco but nothing else unluckily one can not visit loafer in the serai by day friend buy horse would not understand it consequently be oblige to see mcintosh after dark he laugh at this and say simply:--“you be perfectly right when enjoy position in society rather high than your should have do exactly the same thing good heaven be once”--he speak as though he have fall from the command of regiment--“an oxford man this account for the reference to charley symonds stable you say mcintosh slowly have not have that advantage but to outward appearance you do not seem possess of craving for strong drink on the whole fancy that you be the lucky of the two yet be not certain you be forgive my saying so even while be smoke your excellent tobacco painfully ignorant of many thing we be sit together on the edge of his bedstead for he own no chair watch the horse be water for the night while the native woman be prepare dinner do not like be patronize by loafer but be his guest for the time be though he own only one very torn alpaca coat and pair of trouser make out of gunny bag he take the pipe out of his mouth and go on judicially:--“all thing consider doubt whether you be the lucky do not refer to your extremely limited classical attainment or your excruciating quantity but to your gross ignorance of matter more immediately under your notice that for instance --he point to woman clean samovar near the well in the centre of the serai she be flick the water out of the spout in regular cadence jerk there be way and way of clean samovar if you know why she be do her work in that particular fashion you would know what the spanish monk mean when he said-- the trinity illustrate drink water orange pulp-- in three sip the aryan frustrate while he drain his at one gulp.-- and many other thing which now be hide from your eye however mrs mcintosh have prepare dinner let we come and eat after the fashion of the people of the country of whom by the way you know nothing the native woman dip her hand in the dish with we this be wrong the wife should always wait until the husband have eat mcintosh jellaludin apologize saying:--
it be an english prejudice which have not be able to overcome and she love why have never be able to understand fore gather with she at jullundur three year ago and she have remain with ever since believe she to be moral and know she to be skilled in cookery he pat the woman 's head as he speak
and she coo softly she be not pretty to look at mcintosh never tell what position he have hold before his fall he be when sober scholar and gentleman when drunk he be rather more of the first than the second he use to get drunk about once week for two day on those occasion the native woman tend he while he rave in all tongue except his own one day indeed he begin recite atalanta in calydon and go through it to the end beat time to the swing of the verse with bedstead leg
but he do most of his raving in greek or german the man 's mind be perfect rag bag of useless thing once when he be begin to get sober he tell that be the only rational being in the inferno into which he have descend virgil in the shade he say and that in return for my tobacco he would before he die give the material of new inferno that should make great than dante then he fall asleep on horse blanket and wake up quite calm man say he when you have reach the uttermost depth of degradation little incident which would vex high life be to you of no consequence last night my soul be among the god but make no doubt that my bestial body be writhe down here in the garbage you be abominably drunk if that be what you mean say be drunk filthy drunk who be the son of man with whom you have no concern who be once fellow of college whose buttery hatch you have not see be loathsomely drunk but consider how lightly be touch it be nothing to less than nothing for do not even feel the headache which should be my portion now in high life how ghastly would have be my punishment how bitter my repentance believe my friend with the neglect education the high be as the low always suppose each degree extreme he turn round on the blanket put his head between his fist and continued:-- on the soul which have lose and on the conscience which have kill tell you that can not feel be as the god know good and evil but untouched by either be this enviable or be it not when man have lose the warning of next morning 's head he must be in bad state answer look at mcintosh on the blanket with his hair over his eye and his lip blue white that do not think the insensibility good enough for pity 's sake do not say that tell you it be good and most enviable think of my consolation
have you so many then mcintosh certainly your attempt at sarcasm which be essentially the weapon of cultured man be crude first my attainment my classical and literary knowledge blur perhaps by immoderate drinking which remind that before my soul go to the god last night sell the pickering horace you so kindly lend ditta mull the clothesman have it it fetch ten anna and may be redeem for rupee but still infinitely superior to yours secondly the abide affection of mrs mcintosh good of wife thirdly monument more enduring than brass which have build up in the seven year of my degradation he stop here and crawl across the room for drink of water he be very shaky and sick he refer several time to his treasure”--some great possession that he own but hold this to be the raving of drink he be as poor and as proud as he could be his manner be not pleasant
but he know enough about the native among whom seven year of his life have be spend to make his acquaintance worth have he use actually to laugh at strickland as an ignorant man--“ignorant west and east”--he say his boast be first that he be an oxford man of rare and shin part which may or may not have be true do not know enough to check his statement and secondly that he have his hand on the pulse of native life”--which be fact as an oxford man he strike as prig he be always throw his education about as mahommedan faquir as mcintosh jellaludin he be all that want for my own end he smoke several pound of my tobacco and teach several ounce of thing worth know
but he would never accept any gift not even when the cold weather come and grip the poor thin chest under the poor thin alpaca coat he grow very angry and say that have insult he and that he be not go into hospital he have live like beast and he would die rationally like man as matter of fact he die of pneumonia and on the night of his death send over grubby note ask to come and help he to die the native woman be weep by the side of the bed mcintosh wrap in cotton cloth be too weak to resent fur coat be throw over he he be very active as far as his mind be concern and his eye be blaze when he have abuse the doctor who come with so foully that the indignant old fellow leave he curse for few minute and calm down then he tell his wife to fetch out the book from hole in the wall she bring out big bundle wrap in the tail of petticoat of old sheet of miscellaneous note paper all number and cover with fine cramped writing mcintosh plough his hand through the rubbish and stir it up lovingly this he say be my work the book of mcintosh jellaludin show what he see and how he live and what befall he and other be also an account of the life and sin and death of mother maturin what mirza murad ali beg 's book be to all other book on native life will my work be to mirza murad ali beg 's this as will be concede by any one who know mirza ali beg 's book be sweeping statement the paper do not look specially valuable but mcintosh handle they as if they be currency note then he say slowly:--“in despite the many weakness of your education you have be good to will speak of your tobacco when reach the god owe you much thank for many kindness but abominate indebtedness for this reason bequeath to you now the monument more enduring than brass my one book rude and imperfect in part
but oh how rare in other wonder if you will understand it it be gift more honorable than bah where be my brain ramble to you will mutilate it horribly you will knock out the gem you call latin quotation you philistine
and you will butcher the style to carve into your own jerky jargon but you can not destroy the whole of it bequeath it to you my brain again mrs mcintosh bear witness that give the sahib all these paper they would be of no use to you heart of my heart and lie it upon you he turn to here that you do not let my book die in its present form it be yours unconditionally the story of mcintosh jellaludin which be not the story of mcintosh jellaludin but of great man than he and of far great woman listen now be neither mad nor drunk that book will make you famous say thank you as the native woman put the bundle into my arm my only baby say mcintosh with smile he be sink fast but he continue to talk as long as breath remain wait for the end
know that in six case out of ten the die man call for his mother he turn on his side and said:-- say how it come into your possession no one will believe you but my name at least will live you will treat it brutally know you will some of it must go the public be fool and prudish fool be their servant once but do your mangling gently very gently it be great work and have pay for it in seven year damnation his voice stop for ten or twelve breath
and then he begin mumble prayer of some kind in greek the native woman cry very bitterly lastly he rise in bed and say as loudly as slowly:--“not guilty my lord then he fall back and the stupor hold he till he die the native woman run into the serai among the horse and scream and beat her breast for she have love he perhaps his last sentence in life tell what mcintosh have once go through but save the big bundle of old sheet in the cloth there be nothing in his room to say who or what he have be the paper be in hopeless muddle strickland help to sort they
and he say that the writer be either an extreme liar or most wonderful person he think the former one of these day you may be able to judge for yourself the bundle need much expurgation and be full of greek nonsense at the head of the chapter which have all be cut out if the thing be ever publish some one may perhaps remember this story now print as safeguard to prove that mcintosh jellaludin and not myself write the book of mother maturin do not want the giant 's robe to come true in my case the jungle book by rudyard kipling contents mowgli ’s brother hunting song of the seeonee pack kaa ’s hunt road song of the bandar log tiger mowgli ’s song the white seal lukannon rikki tikki tavi darzee ’s chant toomai of the elephant shiv and the grasshopper her majesty ’s servant parade song of the camp animal mowgli ’s brother now rann the kite bring home the night that mang the bat set free-- the herd be shut in byre and hut for loosed till dawn be we this be the hour of pride and power talon and tush and claw
oh hear the call!--good hunt all that keep the jungle law night song in the jungle it be seven o’clock of very warm evening in the seeonee hill when father wolf wake up from his day ’s rest scratch himself yawn and spread out his paw one after the other to get rid of the sleepy feeling in their tip mother wolf lie with her big gray nose drop across her four tumbling squeal cub and the moon shine into the mouth of the cave where they all live augrh say father wolf it be time to hunt again he be go to spring down hill when little shadow with bushy tail cross the threshold and whine good luck go with you chief of the wolf and good luck and strong white tooth go with noble child that they may never forget the hungry in this world it be the jackal tabaqui the dish licker and the wolf of india despise tabaqui because he run about make mischief and tell tale and eat rag and piece of leather from the village rubbish heap
but they be afraid of he too because tabaqui more than anyone else in the jungle be apt to go mad and then he forget that he be ever afraid of anyone and run through the forest bite everything in his way even the tiger run and hide when little tabaqui go mad for madness be the most disgraceful thing that can overtake wild creature we call it hydrophobia but they call it dewanee the madness and run enter then and look say father wolf stiffly but there be no food here for wolf no say tabaqui but for so mean person as myself dry bone be good feast who be we the gidur log the jackal people to pick and choose he scuttle to the back of the cave where he find the bone of buck with some meat on it and sit crack the end merrily all thank for this good meal he say lick his lip how beautiful be the noble child how large be their eye and so young too indeed indeed might have remember that the child of king be man from the beginning now tabaqui know as well as anyone else that there be nothing so unlucky as to compliment child to their face
it please he to see mother and father wolf look uncomfortable tabaqui sit still rejoice in the mischief that he have make and then he say spitefully shere khan the big one have shift his hunting ground he will hunt among these hill for the next moon
