so mulvaney shut down lid and say ye'll be careful marm whin ye tek he out he be disaccustome to travel by railway
an he will be sure to want his rale mistress an his friend learoyd so ye'll make allowance for his feeling at fost she would do all thot an more for the dear good rip an
she would nut oppen basket till they be mile away for fear anybody should recognise he an we be real good and kind soldier man we be an she bond bundle note an then coom up few of her relation an friend to say good by not more than seventy five there be not an we cut away what coom to three hundred and fifty rupee thot be what can scarcelin tell yo but we melt it we melt it it be share an share alike for mulvaney say if learoyd get hold of mrs desussa first sure twa that renumbere the sargint 's dog just in the nick av time an orth'ris be the artist av janius that make work av art out av that ugly piece av ill nature yet by way av thank offerin that be not lead into felony by that wicked ould woman will send thrifle to father victor for the poor people he be always beggin for but an orth'ris he bein cockney an bein pretty far north do nut see it saame way we would getten brass an we mean to keep it an soa we do for short time
noa noa we niver heere wod more awd lass our rig'mint go to pindi an canteen sargint he get himself another tyke insteead one at get lose so reg'lar an be lose for good at last the big drunk draf we be go ome we be go ome-- our ship be at the shore an you mus pack your aversack for we will not come back no more ho do not you grieve for my lovely mary ann for will many you yet on fourp'ny bit as time expire ma an barrack room ballad an awful thing have happen my friend private mulvaney who go home in the serapis time expire not very long ago have come back to india as civilian it be all dinah shadd 's fault she could not stand the poky little lodging
and she miss her servant abdullah more than word could tell the fact be that the mulvaney have be out here too long and have lose touch of england mulvaney know contractor on one of the new central india line and write to he for some sort of work the contractor say that if mulvaney could pay the passage he would give he command of gang of cooly for old sake 's sake the pay be eighty five rupee month and dinah shadd say that if terence do not accept she would make his life baste purgathory therefore the mulvaney come out as civilian which be great and terrible fall though mulvaney try to disguise it by say that he be ker'nel on the railway line an consequinshal man he write an invitation on tool indent form to visit he and come down to the funny little construction bungalow at the side of the line dinah shadd have plant pea about and about and nature have spread all manner of green stuff round the place there be no change in mulvaney except the change of clothing which be deplorable but could not be help he be stand upon his trolly harangue gangman and his shoulder be as well drill and his big thick chin be as clean shaven as ever be civilian now say mulvaney cud you tell that be iver martial man do not answer sorr av you be strainin betune compliment an lie there be no houldin dinah shadd now she be get house av her own go inside an dhrink tay out av chiny in the drrrrawin'-room an thin we will dhrink like christians undher the tree
here scutt ye naygur folk there be sahib come to call on an that be more than he will iver do for you onless you run get out an go on pilin up the earth quick till sundown when we three be comfortably settle under the big sisham in front of the bungalow and the first rush of question and answer about private ortheris and learoyd and old time and place have die away mulvaney say reflectively--'glory be there be no p'rade to morrow an no bun head corp'ril bhoy to give you his lip an yit do not know tis harrd to be something ye niver be an niver mean to be an all the ould day shut up along wid your paper be growin rusty an tis the will av god that man must not serve his quane for time an all he help himself to fresh peg and sigh furiously let your beard grow mulvaney say
and then you will not be trouble with those notion you will be real civilian dinah shadd have tell in the drawing room of her desire to coax mulvaney into let his beard grow twas so civilian like say poor dinah who hat her husband 's hankering for his old life dinah shadd you be dishgrace to an honust clanescraped man say mulvaney without reply to grow beard on your own chin darlint and lave my razor alone they be all that stand betune and dis ris pect ability av do not shave wud be torminte wid an outraji thurrst for there be nothing so dhryin to the throat as big billy goat beard waggin undher the chin ye wudn't have dhrink always dinah shadd by the same token you be kapin crool dhry now let look at that whiskey the whiskey be lend and return
but dinah shadd who have be just as eager as her husband in ask after old friend rent with-- take shame for you sorr come down here though the saint know you be as welkim as the daylight whin
you do come an upsettin terence 's head wid your nonsense about about
fwhat be much well forget he bein civilian now an you niver be aught else can you not let the arrmy rest tis not good for terence take refuge by mulvaney for dinah shadd have temper of her own let be let be say mulvaney tis only wanst in way can talk about the ould day then to me:--'ye say dhrumshtick be well an his lady tu niver know how like the gray garron till be shut av he an asia --'dhrumshtick be the nickname of the colonel commanding mulvaney 's old
regiment.--'will you be seein he again you will thin tell him'--mulvaney 's eye begin to twinkle--'tell he wid privit--
mister terence interrupt dinah shadd now the divil an all his angil an the firmament av hiven fly away wid the mister
an the sin av make swear be on your confession dinah shadd privit tell ye wid privit mulvaney 's good obedience that but for the last time expire wud be still pullin hair on their way to the sea he throw himself back in the chair chuckle and be silent mrs mulvaney say please take up the whiskey and do not let he have it until he have tell the story dinah shadd dexterously whip the bottle away say at the same time tis nothing to be proud av and thus capture by the enemy mulvaney spake:-- twas on chuseday week be behaderin round wid the gang on the bankmint have teach the hopper how to kape step an stop screechin'--whin head gangman come up to wid two inch av shirt tail hang round his neck an disthressful light in his oi sahib sez he there be rig'mint an half av soldier up at the junction knockin red cinder out av ivrythe an ivrybody they thrie to hang in my cloth he sez an there will be murder an ruin an rape in the place before nightfall they say they be comin down here to wake we up what will we do wid our woman folk fetch my throlly sez my heart 's sick in my rib for wink at anything wid the quane 's uniform on ut fetch my throlly an six av the jildiest man and run up in shtyle he tuk his good coat say dinah shadd reproachfully twas to do honour to the widdy cud ha do no less dinah shadd you and your digresshin interfere wid the coorse av the narrative have you iver considhere
fwhat wud look like wid head shave as well as my chin you bear that in your mind dinah darlin be throllie up six mile all to get shquint at that draf know twa spring draf go home for there be no rig'mint hereabout more be the pity praise the virgin murmur dinah shadd but mulvaney do not hear whin be about three quarter av mile off the rest camp powtherin along fit to burrst hear the noise av the man an on my sowl
sorr cud catch the voice av peg barney bellowin like bison wid the belly ache you remimber peg barney that be in comp'ny red hairy scraun wid scar on his jaw peg barney that clear out the blue lights jubilee meeting wid the cook room mop
last year thin know ut be draf of the ould rig'mint an be conshume wid sorrow for the bhoy that be in charge we be harrd scrapin be at any time
do iver tell you how horker kelley go into clink nakid as phoebus apollonius wid the shirt av the corp'ril an file undher his arrum an he be moild man but be digreshin tis shame both to the rig'mint and the arrmy sendin down little orf'cer bhoy wid draf av strong man mad wid liquor an the chanst av gettin shut av india an niver punishment that be fit to be give right down an away from cantonmint to the dock tis this nonsince whin be servin my time be undher the article av war an can be whip on the peg for thim but whin have serve my time be reserve man an the article av war have not any hould on an orf'cer can not do anythin to time expire savin confinin he to barrick tis wise rig'lation bekaze time expire do not have any barrick bein on the move all the time tis solomon av rig'lation be that wud like to be inthroduce to the man that make ut tis easy to get colt from kibbereen horse fair into galway than to take bad draf over ten mile av country consiquintly that rig'lation for fear that the man wud be hurt by the little orf'cer bhoy no matther the near my throlly come to the rest camp the woilder be the shine an the loud be the voice av peg barney tis good be here think to myself for peg alone be employmint for two or three he bein well know as cop as dhrover faith that rest camp be sight the tent rope be all skew nosed an the peg look as dhrunk as the man fifty av thim the scourin 's an rinsin 's an divil 's lavin 's av the ould rig'mint tell you sorr they be dhrunker than any man you have ever see in your mortial life how do draf get dhrunk how do frog get fat they suk ut in through their shkin there be peg barney sittin on the groun in his shirt wan shoe off an wan shoe on whackin tent peg over the head wid his boot an singin fit to wake the dead twas
no clane song that he sing though twa the divil 's mass
what be that whin bad egg be shut av the arrmy he sing the divil 's mass for good riddance
an that mane swearin at ivrythe from the commandher in chief down to the room corp'ril such as you niver in your day hear some man can swear so as to make green turf crack have you iver hear the curse in an orange lodge the divil 's mass be ten time bad an peg barney be singin ut whackin the tent peg on the head wid his boot for each man that he curse powerful big voice have peg barney an hard swearer he be whin
sober stand forninst he an twa not oi alone that cud tell peg be dhrunk as coot good mornin peg sez whin he dhrew breath
afther cursin
the adj'tint gen'ral have put on my good coat to see you peg barney sez i. thin take ut off again sez peg barney latherin away wid the boot take ut off an dance ye lousy civilian wid that he begin cursin ould dhrumshtick be so full he clean disremimber the brigade major an the judge advokit gen'ral do you know peg sez though blood be hot in wid be call civilian an he decent married man wail dinah shadd do not sez peg but dhrunk or sober will tear the hide off your back wid shovel whin have stop singin say you so peg barney sez i. tis clear as mud you have forget will assist your autobiography wid that stretch peg barney boot an all an wint into the camp an awful sight ut be where be the orf'cer in charge av the detachment sez to scrub greene the man little worm that ever walk there be no orf'cer ye ould cook sez scrub we be bloomin republic be you that sez thin be o'connell the dictator an by this you will larn to kape civil tongue in your rag box wid that stretch scrub greene an wint to the orf'cer 's tent twa new little bhoy not wan would iver see before he be sittin in his tent purtendin not to ave ear av the racket salute but for the life av mint to shake hand go in twas the sword hangin on the tentpole change my will can not help sorr sez tis strong man 's job they have give you an you will be wantin help by sundown he be bhoy wid bowil that child an rale gintleman sit down sez
he not before my orf'cer sez an tould he fwhat my service be have hear av you sez he you tuk the town av lungtungpen nakid faith think that be honour an glory for twa lift'nint brazenose do that job be wid ye sorr sez if be av use they shud niver ha send you down wid the draf savin your presince
sorr sez tis only lift'nint hackerston in the ould rig'mint can manage home draf
have niver have charge of man like this before sez he playin wid the pen on the table an see by the rig'lations-- shut your oi to the rig'lations sorr sez till the throoper be into blue wather by the rig'lation you have get to tuck thim up for the night or they will be runnin foul av my cooly an makin shiverarium half through the country can you trust your non com
yes sez
he good sez there will be throuble before the night be you marchin sorr to the next station sez he well still sez there will be big throuble can not be too hard on home draf sez
he the great thing be to get thim in ship faith you have larnt the half av your lesson
sorr sez
but av you shtick to the rig'lation you will niver get thim in ship at all at all or there will not be rag av kit betune thim whin you do
twa dear little orf'cer bhoy an by way av kapin his heart up tould he see wanst in draf in egypt what be that mulvaney say i. sivin an fifty man sittin on the bank av canal laughin at poor little squidgereen av an orf'cer that they have make wade into the slush an pitch the thing out av the boat for their lord high mightiness that make orf'cer bhoy woild with indignation soft an aisy
sorr sez you have niver have your draf in hand since you leave cantonmint wait till the night an your work will be ready to you wid your permission sorr will investigate the camp an talk to my ould frind tis no manner av use thryin to shtop the divilment now wid that wint out into the camp an inthrojuced mysilf to ivry man sober enough to remimber be some wan in the ould day an the bhoy be glad to see all excipt peg barney wid eye like tomata five day in the bazar an nose to match they come round an shuk an tould thim be in privit employ wid an income av own an drrrawin'-room fit to bate the quane 's an wid lie an shtorie an nonsinse gin'rally keep they quiet in wan way an another knockin roun the camp twas bad even thin whin be the angil av peace talk to ould non coms--_they be sober an betune an thim we wear the draf over into their tent at the proper time the little orf'cer bhoy he come round decint an civil speak as might be rough quarter man
he but you can not look to be as comfortable as in barrick we must make the good av thing have shut my eye to dale av dog 's trick today an now there must be no more av ut
no more we will come an have dhrink son sez peg barney staggerin where he stud little orf'cer bhoy kep his timper you be sulky swine you be sez peg barney an at that the man in the tent begin to laugh
tould you orf'cer bhoy have bowil he cut peg barney as near as might be on the oi that would squshe whin we first meet peg wint spinnin acrost the tent peg he out sorr sez in whishper peg he out sez orf'cer bhoy up loud just as if twa battalion p'rade an he pickin his wurrd from the sargint the non com tuk peg barney
howlin handful he be an in three minute he be peg out chin down tight dhrawn on his stummick tent peg to each arm an leg swearin fit to turn naygur white tuk peg an jammed ut into his ugly jaw.--“bite on that peg barney sez the night be settin frosty
an you will be wantin divarsion before the mornin but for the rig'lation you would be bitin on bullet now at the thriangle peg barney sez i. all the draf be out av their tent watchin barney bein peg tis agin
the rig'lation he strook he screech out scrub greene who be always lawyer an some of the man tuk up the shoutin peg out that man sez my orf'cer bhoy
niver losin his timper an the non com wint in and peg out scrub greene by the side av peg barney cud see that the draf be comin roun the man stud not knowin fwhat to do get to your tent sez orf'cer bhoy sargint put sintry over these two man the man wint back into the tent like jackal an the rest av the night there be no noise at all excipt the stip av the sintry over the two an scrub greene blubberin like child twas chilly night an faith ut sober peg barney just before revelly my orf'cer bhoy come out an sez loose those man an send thim to their tent scrub greene wint away widout word but peg barney stiff wid the cowld stud like sheep thryin to make his orf'cer understhand he be sorry for playin the goat there be no tucker in the draf whin ut fall in for the march an divil wurrd about illegality cud hear wint to the ould colour sargint and sez:--“let die in glory sez i. have see man this day man he be sez ould hother the draf 's as sick as herrin they will all go down to the sea like lamb that bhoy have the bowil av cantonmint av gin'ral amin sez an good luck go wid
he wheriver he be by land or by sea let know how the draf get clear
an do you know how they do that bhoy so be tould by letter from bombay bullydamne they down to the dock till they cudn't call their sowls their own from the time they leave oi till they be tween deck not wan av thim be more than dacintly dhrunk an by the holy articles av war whin they wint aboard they cheer he till they cudn't spake an that mark you have not come about wid draf in the mim'ry av livin man you look to that little orf'cer bhoy he have bowil tis not ivry child that wud chuck the rig'lation to flanders an stretch peg barney on wink from brokin an dilapidated ould carkiss like mesilf would be proud to serve-- terence you be civilian say dinah shadd warningly so be so be be ut likely wud forget ut
but he be gran bhoy all the same an be only mud tipper wid hod on my shoulther the whiskey be in the heel
av your hand sorr wid your good lave we will dhrink to the ould rig'mint three finger standin up and we drink the wreck of the visigoth footnote eternal father strong to save whose arm hath bind the restless wave who bidst the mighty ocean keep its own appoint limit deep the lady passenger be try the wheezy old harmonium in front of the cuddy because it be sunday night in the patch of darkness near the wheel grating sit the captain and the end of his cheroot burn like head lamp
there be neither breath nor motion upon the water through which the screw be thud they spread dull silver under the haze of the moonlight till they join the low coast of malacca away to the eastward the voice of the singer at the harmonium be hold down by the awning and come to we with force
oh hear we when we cry to thee for those in peril on the sea it be as though the little congregation be afraid of the vastness of the sea but laugh follow
and some one say shall we take it through again little quick then the captain tell the story of just such night lower his voice for fear of disturb the music and the mind of the passenger she be the visigoth_,--five hundred ton or it may have be six,--in the coast trade one of the good steamer and well find on the kutch kasauli line she be not six year old when the thing happen on just such night as this with an oily smooth sea under brilliant starlight about hundred mile from land to this day
no one know really what the matter be she be so small that she could not have strike even log in the water without every soul on board feel the jar and even if she have strike something it would not have make she go down as she do be fourth officer then we have about seven saloon passenger include the captain 's wife and another woman and perhaps five hundred deck passenger go up the coast to shrine on just such night as this when she be rip through the level sea at level nine knot an hour the man on the bridge whoever it be see that she be sink at the head sink by the head as she go along that be the only warning we get she begin to sink as she go along of course the captain be tell and he send to wake up the saloon passenger and tell they to come on deck sound curious sort of message that to deliver on dead still night the people tumble up in their dressing gown and pyjama and would not believe we be just sink as fast as we could and have to tell they that then the deck passenger get wind of it and all hell wake up along the deck the rule in these little affair be to get your saloon passenger off first then to fill the boat with the balance and afterwards god help the extra that be all be get the starboard stern boat the mail boat away it hang as it might be over yonder and as come along from the cuddy the deck passenger hang round shove their money belt into my hand take off their nose ring and earring and thrust they upon to buy just one chance for life if have not be so desperately busy should have think it horrible put biscuit and water into the boat and get the two lady in one of they be the captain 's wife she have to be put in by main force you have no notion how woman can struggle the other woman be the wife of an officer go to meet her husband and there be couple of passenger beside the lascar the captain say he be go to stay with the ship you see the rule in these affair believe be that the captain have to bow gracefully from the bridge and go down have not have ship under my charge wreck yet when that come will have to do like the other after the boat be away and see that there be nothing to be get by wait jump overboard exactly as might have vault over into flat green field and strike out for the mail boat another officer do the same thing
but he go for boat full of native and they whack he on the chest with oar so he have some difficulty in climb in it be as well that reach the mail boat there be compass in it
but the idiot have manage to fill the boat half full of water somehow or another and none of the crew seem to know what be require of they then the visigoth go down and take every one with she ship generally do that the corp do not cumber the sea for some time what do do keep all the boat together and head into the track of the coast steamer the aggravating thing be the thought that we be close to land as far as big steamer be concern and in the middle of eternity as far as regard little boat the sea look hugeous big from boat at night
oh christ whose voice the water hear and hush their raving at thy word who walkedst on the foam deep and calm amidst its rage
do keep,--
oh hear we when we cry to thee for those in peril on the sea sing the passenger cheerily that harmonium be disgracefully out of tune say the captain the sea air affect their inside well as be say we settle down in the boat the captain 's wife be unconscious she lie in the bottom of the boat and moan be glad she be not thresh about the boat
but what do think be wrong be the way the two man passenger behave they be useless with funk out and out fear they lie in the boat and do nothing fetch groan now and again to show they be alive but that be all but the other woman be jewel
damn it
it be worth be shipwreck to have that woman in the boat she be awfully handsome and as brave as she be lovely she help bail out the boat and she work like man so we kick about the sea from midnight till seven the next evening
and then we see steamer
will will give you anything be wear to hoist as signal of distress say the woman but have no need to ask she for the steamer pick we up and take we back to bombay forget to tell you that when the day break could not recognise the captain 's wife widow mean she have change in the night as if fire have go over she meet she long time afterwards and even then she have not forgive for put she into the boat and obey the captain 's order but the husband of the other woman he be in the army write no end of letter of thank do not suppose he consider that the way his wife behave be enough to make any decent man do all he could the other fellow who lie in the bottom of the boat and groan have never meet do not want to should not be civil to they if do and that be how the visigoth go down for no assignable reason with eighty bag of mail five hundred soul and not single packet insure on just such night as this oh trinity of love and power our brother shield in that dread hour from rock and tempest fire and foe protect they whereso'er they go thus evermore shall rise to thee glad hymn of praise by land and sea strike they will go on sing that hymn all night imperfect sort of doctrine in the last line do not you think they might have run in an extra verse specify sudden collapse like the visigoth 's be go on to the bridge now good night say the captain and be leave alone with the steady thud thud of the screw and the gentle creaking of the boat at the davit that make shudder the solid muldoon do ye see john malone wid his shinin brand new hat do ye see how he walk like grand aristocrat there be flag an banner wavin high an dhress and shtyle be show
but the good av all the company be misther john malone john malone there have be royal dog fight in the ravine at the back of the rifle butt between learoyd 's jock and ortheris 's blue rot_--both mongrel rampur hound chiefly rib and tooth it last for twenty happy howl minute and then blue rot collapse and ortheris pay learoyd three rupee and we be all very thirsty dog fight be most heating entertainment quite apart from the shouting because rampur fight over couple of acre of ground later when the sound of belt badge click against the neck of beer bottle have die away conversation drift from dog to man fight of all kind human resemble red deer in some respect any talk of fighting seem to wake up sort of imp in their breast
and they bell one to the other exactly like challenge buck this be noticeable even in man who consider themselves superior to private of the line it show the refining influence of civilisation and the march of progress tale provoke tale and each tale more beer even dreamy learoyd 's eye begin to brighten and he unburden himself of long history in which trip to malham cove girl at pateley brigg ganger himself and pair of clogs be mix in drawl tangle an so ah coot 's yead oppen from chin to hair
an he be abe for matter month conclude learoyd pensively mulvaney come out of reverie he be lie down and flourish his heel in the air you be man learoyd say he critically
but you have only fight wid man an that be an ivry day expayrience but have stud up to ghost an that be not an ivry day expayrience
no say ortheris throw cork at he you git up an address the ouse you an yer expayrience be it big one nor usual twas the livin trut answer mulvaney stretch out huge arm and catch ortheris by the collar now where be ye son will ye take the wurrud av the lorrd out av my mouth another time he shake he to emphasise the question no something else though say ortheris make dash at mulvaney 's pipe capture it and hold it at arm 's length will chuck it acrost the ditch if you do not let go you maraudin hathen tis the only cutty iver love handle her tinder or will chuck you acrost the nullah if that poipe be bruk ah give she back to sorr ortheris have pas the treasure to my hand it be an absolutely perfect clay as shiny as the black ball at pool take it reverently but be firm will you tell we about the ghost fight if do be ut the shtory that be troublin you av course will mint to all along be only gettin at ut my own way as popp doggle say whin they find he thrye to ram cartridge down the muzzle orth'ris fall away he release the little londoner take back his pipe fill it and his eye twinkle he have the most eloquent eye of any one that know do iver tell you he begin that be wanst the divil av man you do say learoyd with childish gravity that make ortheris yell with laughter for mulvaney be always impress upon we his great merit in the old day do iver tell you mulvaney continue calmly that be wanst more av divil than be now mer ria you do not mean it say ortheris whin be corp'ril be rejuce aftherward
but as say whin be corp'ril be divil of man he be silent for nearly minute while his mind rummage among old memory and his eye glow he bite upon the pipe stem and charge into his tale they be great time be ould now hide 's wear off in patch sinthrygo have disconceite an be married man tu but have have my day have have my day an nothing can take away the taste av that oh my time past whin put fut through ivry livin wan av the tin commandmint between revelly and light out blow the froth off pewter wipe moustache wid the back av hand an sleep on ut all as quiet as little child
but ut be over ut be over an twill niver come back to not though pray for week av sundays be there any wan in the ould rig'mint to touch corp'ril
terence mulvaney whin that same be turn out for sedukshin niver meet he ivry woman that be not witch be worth the runnin afther in those day an ivry man be my dear frind or have strip to he an we know which be the betther av the tu whin be corp'ril wud not ha change wid the colonel
no nor yet the commandher in chief wud be sargint there be nothing wud not be mother av hivin look at fwhat be now we be quarter in big cantonmint--'tis no manner av use namin name for ut might give the barrick disrepitation an be the imperor av the earth to my own mind an wan or tu woman think the same small blame to thim afther we have lie there year bragin the colour sargint av comp'ny wint an take wife that be lady 's maid to some big lady in the station she be dead now be annie bragin die in child bed at kirpa tal or ut may ha be almorah seven nine year go an bragin he marry agin
but she be pretty woman whin bragin inthrojuce she to cantonmint society she have eye like the brown av buttherfly 's wing whin the sun catch ut an waist no thick than my arm an little sof button av mouth would ha go through all asia bristlin wid bay'nit to get the kiss av an her hair be as long as the tail av the colonel 's charger forgive mentionin that blunderin baste in the same mouthful with annie bragin but'twas
all shpun gold an time be when lock av ut be more than di'mond to there be niver pretty woman yet an have have thruck wid
few cud open the door to annie bragin twas in the cath'lic chapel see she first oi roll round as usual to see fwhat be to be see you be too good for bragin my love think to mesilf but that be mistake can put straight or my name be not terence mulvaney now take my wurrd for ut
you orth'ris there an learoyd an kape out av the married quarter as do not no good iver come av ut
an there be always the chance av your bein find wid your face in the dirt long picket in the back av your head an your hand play the fife on the tread av another man 's doorstep
so we find o'hara he that rafferty kill six year go when he wint to his death wid his hair oil whistlin
larry o'rourke betune his tooth kape out av the married quarter say as do not tis onwholesim tis dangerous an tis ivrythe else that be bad but my sowl tis swate while ut last be always hangin about there whin be off duty an bragin be not but niver sweet word beyon ordinar do get from annie bragin tis the pervarsity av the sect sez to mesilf an give my cap another cock on my head an straighten my back--'twas the back av dhrum major in those day an wint off as tho do not care wid all the woman in the married quarter laughin be pershuade most bhoy be be thinkin'--that no woman bear av woman cud stand against av hild up my little finger have reason fer thinkin that way till meet annie bragin time an agin whin be blandandherin in the dusk man wud go past as quiet as cat that be quare think for be or should be the only man in these part now what divilment can annie be up to thin call myself blayguard for thinkin such thing but think thim all the same an that mark
you be the way av man wan evenin said:--“mrs bragin manin
no disrespect to you who be that corp'ril man”--i have see the stripe though cud niver get sight av his face--“_who be that corp'ril man that come in always whin be go away
mother av god sez
she turnin as white as my belt have you see he too see he sez av coorse have do ye want not to see he for”--we be standin talkin in the dhark outside the veranda av bragin 's quarters--“you'd betther tell to shut eye onless be mistake he be come now an sure enough the corp'ril be walkin to we hangin his head down as though he be ashamed av himsilf good night
mrs bragin sez very cool tis not for to interfere wid your moor
but you might manage thing wid more dacincy be off to canteen sez turn on my heel an wint away swearin wud give that man dhressin that wud shtop he messin about the married quarter for month an week have not tuk ten pace before annie bragin be hangin on to my arm an cud feel that she be shakin all over stay wid mister mulvaney
she you be flesh an blood at the least be ye not be all that sez
an my anger wint away in flash will want to be ask twice annie wid that slip my arm round her waist for begad fancy
she have surrindere at discretion an the honours av war be mine fwhat nonsinse be this sez she dhrawin hersilf up on the tip av her dear little toe wid the mother 's milk not dhry on your impident mouth let go
she sez do ye not say just now that be flesh an blood sez i. have not change since sez an kep my arm where ut be your arm to yoursilf
sez she an her eye sparkild sure tis only human nature sez

sez she the straight in cantonmint wid that drop my arm fall back tu pace an salute for see that she mint she say
then you know something that some man would give good deal to be certain of how could you tell demand in the interest of science watch the hand say mulvaney av she shut her hand tight thumb down over the knuckle take up your hat an go you will only make fool av yoursilf av you shtay but av the hand lie opin on the lap or av you see her thryin to shut ut an she can't,--go on she be not past reasonin wid well as be sayin fall back salute an be go away shtay wid she sez he be comin again she point to the veranda an by the hoight av impart'nince the corp'ril man be comin out av bragin 's quarter he be do that these five evenin 's past sez annie bragin
oh fwhat will do he will not do ut again sez for be fightin mad kape away from man that have be thrifle cross in love till the fever 's die down he rag like brute beast wint up to the man in the veranda manin as sure as sit to knock the life out
av he he slip into the open fwhat be you do philanderin about here

niver stop swearin from ind to ind an the only time that learoyd opin his mouth to sing be whin he be messin wid other people 's head for he be dhirty fighter be jock recruitie sometime cry an sometime they do not know fwhat

but some man get heavy dead dhrunk on the fightin this man be he be staggerin an his eye be half shut an we cud hear he dhraw breath twinty yard away he see the little orf'cer bhoy an come up talkin thick an drowsy to himsilf blood the young whelp he sez blood
the young whelp an wid that he throw up his arm shpun roun an drop at our foot dead as paythan an there be niver sign or scratch on he they say twa his heart be rotten
but oh twa quare thing to see thin we wint to bury our dead for we wud not lave thim to the paythan an in movin among the haythen we nearly lose that little orf'cer bhoy he be for givin wan divil wather and layin he aisy against rock be careful sorr sez wind paythan be bad than live wan my troth before the word be out of my mouth the man on the ground fire at the orf'cer bhoy lanin over he an see the helmit fly drop the butt on the face
av the man an tuk his pistol the little orf'cer bhoy turn very white for the hair
av half his head be sing away tould
you so sorr sez an afther that whin
he want to help paythan stud wid the muzzle contagious to the ear they dare not do anythin but curse the tyrone be growlin like dog over bone that have be take away too soon for they have see their dead an they want to kill ivry sowl on the ground crook tould thim that he would blow the hide off any man that misconduct himself but see that ut be the first time the tyrone have iver see their dead do not wondher they be on the sharp tis shameful sight whin first see ut wud niver ha give quarter to any man north of the khaibar no nor woman either for the woman use to come out afther dhark auggrh
well evenshually we bury our dead an tuk away our wound an come over the brow av the hill to see the scotchie an the gurkey take tay with the paythan in bucketsfuls we be gang av dissolute ruffian for the blood have cake the dust an the sweat have cut the cake an our bay'nit be hangin like butcher steel betune ur leg an most av we be mark one way or another staff orf'cer man clean as new rifle rid up an sez what damn scarecrow be you comp'ny av her majesty 's black tyrone an wan av the ould rig'mint sez crook very quiet givin our visitor the flure as twas
oh sez
the staff orf'cer do you dislodge that reserve no sez crook an the tyrone laugh thin fwhat the divil have ye do disthroyed ut sez crook
an he take we on but not before toomey that be in the tyrone sez aloud his voice somewhere in his stummick fwhat in the name av misfortune do this parrit widout tail mane by shtoppin the road av his betther the staff orf'cer wint blue an toomey make he pink by changin to the voice av minowderin woman an sayin come an kiss major dear for husband be at the war an be all alone at the depot the staff orf'cer wint away an cud see crook 's shoulther shakin his corp'ril check toomey lave alone sez toomey widout wink be his batman before he be marry an he know fwhat mane av you do not there be nothing like livin in the hoight av society d'you remimber that orth'ris
hi do toomey die in orspital next week it be 'cause buy arf his kit an remember after that-- guarrd turn out the relief have come it be four o'clock will catch kyart for you sorr say mulvaney dive hastily into his accoutrement come up to the top av the fort an we will pershue our invistigation into m'grath 's shtable the relieved guard stroll round the main bastion on its way to the swimming bath and learoyd grow almost talkative ortheris look into the fort ditch and across the plain ho it be weary waitin for ma ary he hum but would like to kill some more bloomin paythan before my time be up bloody war north east south and west amen say learoyd slowly fwhat be here say mulvaney check at blur of white by the foot of the old sentry box he stoop and touch it it be norah norah m'taggart
why nonie darlin fwhat be ye do out av your mother 's bed at this time the two year old child of sergeant m'taggart must have wander for breath of cool air to the very verge of the parapet of the fort ditch her tiny night shift be gather into wisp round her neck
and she moan in her sleep see there say mulvaney poor lamb look at the heat rash on the innocint skin av she tis hard crool hard even for we fwhat must it be for these wake up nonie your mother will be woild about you begad the child might ha fall into the ditch he pick she up in the grow light and set she on his shoulder and her fair curl touch the grizzled stubble of his temple ortheris and learoyd follow snap their finger while norah smile at they sleepy smile then carol mulvaney clear as lark dance the baby on his arm-- if any young man should marry you say nothing about the joke that iver ye slep in sinthry box wrap up in soldier 's cloak though on my sowl nonie he say gravely there be not much cloak about you niver mind you will not dhress like this ten year to come kiss your friend an run along to your mother nonie set down close to the married quarter nod with the quiet obedience of the soldier 's child
but ere she patter off over the flag path hold up her lip to be kiss by the three musketeer ortheris wipe his mouth with the back of his hand and swear sentimentally learoyd turn pink and the two walk away together the yorkshireman lift up his voice and give in thunder the chorus of the sentry box while ortheris pip at his side bin to bloomin sing song you two say the artilleryman who be take his cartridge down to the morning gun you be over merry for these dash day bid ye take care the brat say he for it come of noble race learoyd bellow the voice die out in the swimming bath
oh terence say drop into mulvaney 's speech when we be alone it be you that have the tongue he look at wearily his eye be sink in his head and his face be draw and white eyah say he have blandandhere thim through the night somehow but can thim that help other help thimselve answer that sorr and over the bastion of fort amara break the pitiless day in the matter of private hurrah soldier 's life for shout boy shout for it make you jolly and free the ramrod corps people who have see say that one of the quaint spectacle of human frailty be an outbreak of hysteric in girl school it start without warn generally on hot afternoon among the elder pupil girl giggle till the giggle get beyond control then she throw up her head and cry honk honk honk like wild goose and tear mix with the laughter if the mistress be wise she will rap out something severe at this point to check matter if she be tender hearted and send for drink of water the chance be largely in favour of another girl laugh at the afflict one and herself collapse thus the trouble spread and may end in half of what answer to the low sixth of boy school rock and whoop together give week of warm weather two stately promenade per diem heavy mutton and rice meal in the middle of the day certain amount of nagging from the teacher and few other thing some amazing effect develop at least this be what folk say who have have experience now the mother superior of convent and the colonel of british infantry regiment would be justly shock at any comparison be make between their respective charge
but it be fact that under certain circumstance thomas in bulk can be work up into ditthere ripple hysteria he do not weep but he show his trouble unmistakably and the consequence get into the newspaper and all the good people who hardly know martini from snider say take away the brute 's ammunition thomas be not brute and his business which be to look after the virtuous people demand that he shall have his ammunition to his hand he do not wear silk stocking and he really ought to be supply with new adjective to help he to express his opinion but for all that he be great man if you call he the heroic defender of the national honour one day and brutal and licentious soldiery the next you naturally bewilder he and he look upon you with suspicion there be nobody to speak for thomas except people who have theory to work off on he and nobody understand thomas except thomas
and he do not always know what be the matter with himself that be the prologue this be the story:-- corporal slane be engage to be married to miss jhansi m'kenna whose history be well know in the regiment and elsewhere he have his colonel 's permission and be popular with the man every arrangement have be make to give the wedding what private ortheris call eeklar it fall in the heart of the hot weather and after the wedding slane be go up to the hill with the bride none the less slane 's grievance be that the affair would be only hire carriage wedding
and he feel that the eeklar of that be meagre miss m'kenna do not care so much the sergeant 's wife be help she to make her wedding dress
and she be very busy slane be just then the only moderately contented man in barrack all the rest be more or less miserable and they have so much to make they happy too all their work be over at eight in the morning and for the rest of the day they could lie on their back and smoke canteen plug and swear at the punkah cooly they enjoy fine full flesh meal in the middle of the day and then throw themselves down on their cot and sweat and sleep till it be cool enough to go out with their towny whose vocabulary contain less than six hundred word and the adjective and whose view on every conceivable question they have hear many time before there be the canteen of course and there be the temperance room with the second hand paper in it but man of any profession can not read for eight hour day in temperature of degree or degree in the shade run up sometimes to degree at midnight very few man even though they get pannikin of flat stale muddy beer and hide it under their cot can continue drink for six hour day one man try
but he die and nearly the whole regiment go to his funeral because it give they something to do it be too early for the excitement of fever or cholera the man could only wait and wait and wait and watch the shadow of the barrack creep across the blind white dust that be gay life they lounge about cantonment it be too hot for any sort of game and almost too hot for vice and fuddle themselves in the evening and fill themselves to distension with the healthy nitrogenous food provide for they and the more they stoke the less exercise they take and more explosive they grow then temper begin to wear away and man fall brood over insult real or imaginary for they have nothing else to think of the tone of the repartee change and instead of say light heartedly will knock your silly face in man grow laboriously polite and hint that the cantonment be not big enough for themselves and their enemy and that there would be more space for one of the two in another place it may have be the devil who arrange the thing but the fact of the case be that losson have for long time be worry simmon in an aimless way it give he occupation the two have their cot side by side and would sometimes spend long afternoon swear at each other but simmons be afraid of losson and dare not challenge he to fight he think over the word in the hot still night and half the hate he feel towards losson he vent on the wretched punkah coolie losson buy parrot in the bazar and put it into little cage and lower the cage into the cool darkness of well and sit on the well curb shout bad language down to the parrot he teach it to say simmons ye
so oor which mean swine and several other thing entirely unfit for publication he be big gross man and he shake like jelly when the parrot have the sentence correctly simmon however shake with rage for all the room be laugh at he the parrot be such disreputable puff of green feather and it look so human when it chatter losson use to sit swing his fat leg on the side of the cot and ask the parrot what it think of simmons the parrot would answer simmons
ye so oor good boy losson use to say scratch the parrot 's head ye ear that sim and simmons use to turn over on his stomach and make answer ear take eed you do not ear something one of these day in the restless night after he have be asleep all day fit of blind rage come upon simmon and hold he till he tremble all over while he think in how many different way he would slay losson sometimes he would picture himself trample the life out of the man with heavy ammunition boot and at other smash in his face with the butt and at other jump on his shoulder and drag the head back till the neckbone crack then his mouth would feel hot and fevered and he would reach out for another sup of the beer in the pannikin but the fancy that come to he most frequently and stay with he long be one connect with the great roll of fat under lesson 's right ear he notice it first on moonlight night and thereafter it be always before his eye it be fascinating roll of fat man could get his hand upon it and tear away one side of the neck or he could place the muzzle of rifle on it and blow away all the head in flash losson have no right to be sleek and contented and well to do when he simmon be the butt of the room some day perhaps he would show those who laugh at the simmons
ye so oor joke that he be as good as the rest and hold man 's life in the crook of his forefinger when losson snore simmons hat he more bitterly than ever why should losson be able to sleep when simmon have to stay awake hour after hour toss and turn on the tape with the dull liver pain gnaw into his right side and his head throb and ache after canteen he think over this for many many night and the world become unprofitable to he he even blunt his naturally fine appetite with beer and tobacco and all the while the parrot talk at and make mock of he the heat continue and the temper wear away more quickly than before sergeant 's wife die of heat apoplexy in the night and the rumour run abroad that it be cholera man rejoice openly hop that it would spread and send they into camp but that be false alarm it be late on tuesday evening and the man be wait in the deep double veranda for last post when simmon go to the box at the foot of his bed take out his pipe and slam the lid down with bang that echo through the deserted barrack like the crack of rifle ordinarily speak the man would have take no notice but their nerve be fret to fiddle string they jump up and three or four clatter into the barrack room only to find simmon kneel by his box it be you be it they say and laugh foolishly we think twas-- simmons rise slowly if the accident have so shake his fellow what would not the reality do you think it be do you and what make you think he say lash himself into madness as he go on to hell with your thinking ye dirty spy simmons ye so oor chuckle the parrot in the veranda sleepily recognise well know voice now that be absolutely all the tension snap simmon fall back on the arm rack deliberately,--the man be at the far end of the room,--and take out his rifle and packet of ammunition do not go play the goat sim say losson put it down but there be quaver in his voice another man stoop slip his boot and hurl it at simmons 's head the prompt answer be shoot which fire at random find its billet in losson 's throat losson fall forward without word and the other scatter you think it be yell simmons you be drivin to it tell you you be drivin to it get up losson an do not lie shammin there you an your blasted parrit that druv to it
but there be an unaffected reality about losson 's pose that show simmon what he have do the man be still clamour in the veranda simmons appropriate two more packet of ammunition and run into the moonlight mutter will make night of it thirty roun 's an the last for myself take you that you dog he drop on one knee and fire into the brown of the man on the veranda but the bullet fly high and land in the brickwork with vicious phwit that make some of the young one turn pale it be as musketry theorist observe one thing to fire and another to be fire at then the instinct of the chase flare up the news spread from barrack to barrack and the man double out intent on the capture of simmon the wild beast who be head for the cavalry parade ground stop now and again to send back shot and curse in the direction of his pursuer will learn you to spy on he shout will learn you to give dorg 's name come on the ole lot you colonel john anthony deever c.

you be not urt badly sir say slane the major have faint and there be an ugly ragged hole through the top of his arm slane kneel down and murmur s'elp believe be dead well if that be not my bloom luck all over but the major be destine to lead his battery afield for many long day with unshaken nerve he be remove and nurse and pet into convalescence while the battery discuss the wisdom of capture simmon and blow he from gun they idolise their major and his reappearance on parade bring about scene nowhere provide for in the army regulation
great too be the glory that fall to slane 's share the gunner would have make he drunk thrice day for at least fortnight even the colonel of his own regiment compliment he upon his coolness and the local paper call he hero these thing do not puff he up when the major offer he money and thank the virtuous corporal take the one and put aside the other
but he have request to make and preface it with many beg pardon sir could the major see his way to let the slane m'kenna wedding be adorn by the presence of four battery horse to pull hire barouche the major could and so could the battery excessively so it be gorgeous wedding wot do do it for say corporal slane for the orse course jhansi be not beauty to look at but be not go to ave hire turn out jerry blaze if adn't want something sim might ha blow jerry blaze bloom ead into hirish stew for aught would care and they hang private simmon hang he as high as haman in hollow square of the regiment and the colonel say it be drink and the chaplain be sure it be the devil and simmons fancy

av tim o'hara come company all st patrick 's alley be there to see robert buchanan as the three musketeer share their silver tobacco and liquor together as they protect each other in barrack or camp and as they rejoice together over the joy of one so do they divide their sorrow when ortheris be irrepressible tongue have bre he into cell for season or learoyd have run amok through his kit and accoutrement or mulvaney have indulge in strong water and under their influence reprove his command officer you can see the trouble in the face of the untouched two and the rest of the regiment know that comment or jest be unsafe generally the three avoid orderly room and the corner shop that follow leave both to the young blood who have not sow their wild oats but there be occasions-- for instance ortheris be sit on the drawbridge of the main gate of fort amara with his hand in his pocket and his pipe bowl down in his mouth learoyd be lie at full length on the turf of the glacis kick his heel in the air and come round the corner and ask for mulvaney ortheris spit into the ditch and shake his head no good seein now say ortheris be bloomin camel hear on the flag of the veranda opposite to the cell which be close to the guard room measure step that could have identify in the tramp of an army there be twenty pace crescendo pause and then twenty diminuendo that be say ortheris ma son ah've get one rupee eight annas of ma own he show two coin and replace ortheris on the drawbridge rail very good say where be you go to go to walk orf wen come out two mile or three or fower say ortheris the footstep within cease hear the dull thud of knapsack fall on bedstead follow by the rattle of arm ten minute later mulvaney faultlessly dress his lip tight and his face as black as thunderstorm stalk into the sunshine on the drawbridge learoyd and ortheris spring from my side and close in upon he both lean towards as horse lean upon the pole in an instant they have disappear down the sunken road to the cantonment and be leave alone mulvaney have not see fit to recognise so know that his trouble must be heavy upon he climb one of the bastion and watch the figure of the three musketeer grow small and small across the plain they be walk as fast as they could put foot to the ground and their head be bow they fetch great compass round the parade ground skirt the cavalry line and vanish in the belt of tree that fringe the low land by the river follow slowly and sight they dusty sweat but still keep up their long swing tramp on the river bank they crash through the forest reserve head towards the bridge of boat and presently establish themselves on the bow of one of the pontoon ride cautiously till see three puff of white smoke rise and die out in the clear evening air and know that peace have come again at the bridge head they wave forward with gesture of welcome tie up your orse shout ortheris an come on sir we be all go home in this ere bloomin boat from the bridge head to the forest officer 's bungalow be but step the mess man be there and would see that man hold my horse do the sahib require aught else peg or beer ritchie sahib have leave half dozen bottle of the latter but since the sahib be friend of ritchie sahib and he the mess man be poor man--
give my order quietly and return to the bridge mulvaney have take off his boot and be dabble his toe in the water learoyd be lie on his back on the pontoon and ortheris be pretend to row with big bamboo be an ould fool say mulvaney reflectively dhrag gin you two out here bekaze be undher the black dog sulkin like child that be soldierin when mullins an be damn to he be shquealin on counterpin for five shillin week an that not pay bhoy have take you five mile out av natural pevarsity phew
wot be the odd so long as you be appy say ortheris apply himself afresh to the bamboo as well ere as anywhere else learoyd hold up rupee and an eight anna bit and shake his head sorrowfully five mile from canteen all along mulvaney 's blaaste pride know ut say mulvaney penitently why will ye come wid an yet wud be mortial sorry if ye do not any time though be ould enough to know betther but will do penance will take dhrink av wather ortheris squeak shrilly the butler of the forest bungalow be stand near the railing with basket uncertain how to clamber down to the pontoon might know'd you would get liquor out bloomin desert sir say ortheris gracefully to then to the mess man easy with they there bottle they be worth their weight in gold jock ye long armed beggar get out that an hike they down learoyd have the basket on the pontoon in an instant and the three musketeer gather round it with dry lip they drink my health in due and ancient form and thereafter tobacco taste sweet than ever they absorb all the beer and dispose themselves in picturesque attitude to admire the set sun no man speak for while mulvaney 's head drop upon his chest and we think that he be asleep what on earth do you come so far for whisper to ortheris to walk orf course when be be check we allus walk orf be not fit to be speak to those time nor be not fit to leave alone so we take till be mulvaney raise his head and star straight into the sunset have my rifle say he dreamily an have my bay'nit an mullins come round the corner an he look in my face an grin dishpiteful you can not blow your own nose sez he now can not tell fwhat mullins 's expayrience may ha be but mother av god

you know you can do anythin wid whin be talkin do say wud like to cut mullins 's liver out deny the imputashin for fear that orth'ris here wud report ah you wud tip into the river wud you sit quiet mouth but they be always inside the head follow long pause o'hara be divil though save he for the honour av the rig'mint from his death that time say it now he be divil long bould black haired divil which way ask ortheris woman thin know another not more than in reason if you mane ye warp walkin -shtick have be young an for why should not have tuk what cud do iver whin be corp'ril use the rise av my rank wan step an that take away more be the sorrow an the fault av me!--to prosecute nefarious inthrigue as o'hara do do whin be corp'ril lie my spite upon man an make his life dog 's life from day to day do lie as o'hara lie till the young wan in the tyrone turn white wid the fear av the judgment av god killin thim all in lump as ut kill the woman at devize do not have sin my sin an have make my confesshin an father victor know the bad av o'hara be tuk before he cud spake on rafferty 's doorstep
an no man know the bad av he but this much know the tyrone be recruit any fashion in the ould day draf from connemara draf from portsmouth draf from kerry an that be blazin bad draf'--here there and iverywhere but the large av thim be oirish black oirish now there be oirish an oirish the good be good as the good but the bad be wurrst than the wurrst tis this way they clog together in piece as fast as thief
an no wan know fwhat they will do till wan turn informer an the gang be bruk but ut begin again day later meetin in hole an corner an swearin bloody oath an shtickin man in the back an runnin away an thin waitin for the blood money on the reward paper to see if ut be worth enough those be the black oirish an tis they that bring dishgrace upon the name av oireland an thim wud kill as nearly kill wan wanst but to reshume my room--'twas before be marry be wid twelve av the scum av the earth the pickin 's av the gutter mane man that wud neither laugh nor talk nor yet get dhrunk as man shud they thrie some av their dog 's thrick on but dhrew line round my cot an the man that thransgresse ut wint into hospital for three day good o'hara have put his spite on the room he be my colour sargint an nothing cud we do to plaze he be young than be now an tuk what get in the way av dress down and punishment dhrill wid my tongue in my cheek but it be diff'rint wid
the other an why can not say excipt that some man be borrun mane an go to dhirty murdher where fist be more than enough afther whoile they change their chune to an be desp'rit frien'ly all twelve av thim cursin o'hara in chorus eyah sez o'hara 's divil an be not for denyin ut
but be he the only man in the wurruld let he go he will get tired av findin our kit foul an our coutrement onproperly kep we will not let he go sez they thin take he sez an dash poor yield you will get for your throuble be he not misconductin himself wid slimmy 's wife sez another she be common to the rig'mint sez i. fwhat have make ye this partic'lar on suddint have he not put his spite on the roomful av we can we do anythin that he will not check we for sez another that be thrue sez i. will ye not help we to do aught sez another--“a big bould man like you will break his head upon his shoulther av he put hand on sez i. will give he the lie av he say that be dhirty an wud not mind duckin he in the artillery trough if ut be not that be thryin for my shtripe be that all ye will do sez another have ye no more spunk than that ye blood dhrawn calf blood dhrawn may be sez gettin back to my cot an makin my line round ut
but ye know that the man who come acrost this mark will be more blood dhrawn than no man give the name in my mouth sez ondersthand will have no part wid you in anythin
ye do nor will raise my fist to my shuperior be any wan comin on sez i.
they make no move tho give they full time but stud growlin an snarlin together at wan ind av the room tuk up my cap and wint out to canteen thinkin no little av mesilf and there grow most ondacintly dhrunk in my leg my head be all reasonable houligan sez to man in comp'ny that be by way av bein frind av mine be overtuk from the belt down do you give the touch av your shoulther to presarve my formation an march acrost the ground into the high grass will sleep ut off there sez an houligan he be dead now but good he be while he last walk wid givin the touch whin wint
wide ontil
we come to the high grass an my faith the sky an the earth be fair rowlin undher make for where the grass be thickust an there slep off my liquor wid an easy conscience do not desire to come on book too frequent my characther havin' be shpotless for the good half av year whin rouse the dhrink be dyin out in an feel as though she cat have litter in my mouth have not learn to hould my liquor wid comfort in thim day tis little betther be now will get houligan to pour bucket over my head think an wud ha rise but hear some wan say mulvaney can take the blame av ut for the backslidin hound he be oho sez an my head ring like guard room gong fwhat be the blame that this young man must take to oblige tim vulmea for twas tim vulmea that shpoke turn on my belly an crawl through the grass bit at time to where the spache come from there be the twelve av my room sittin down in little patch the dhry grass wavin above their head an the sin av black murdher in their heart put the stuff aside to get clear view fwhat be that sez wan man jumpin up dog say vulmea you be nice hand to this job as say mulvaney will take the blame av ut come to pinch tis harrd to swear man 's life away sez young wan thank ye for that think i. now fwhat the divil be you paragin conthrivin against tis as easy as dhrinkin your quart sez vulmea at seven or thereon o'hara will come acrost to the married quarter go to call on slimmy 's wife the swine wan av us'll pass the wurrd to the room an we shtart the divil an all av shine laughin an crackin on an t'rowin our boot about thin o'hara will come to give we the ordher to be quiet the more by token bekaze the room lamp will be knock over in the larkin he will take the straight road to the ind door where there be the lamp in the veranda an that will bring he clear against the light as he shtand he will not be able to look into the dhark wan av we will loose off an close shot ut will be an shame to the man that miss twill be mulvaney 's rifle
she that be at the head av the rack there be no mistakin that long shtocked cross eyed bitch even in the dhark the thief misname my ould firin'-piece out av jealousy be pershuade av that an ut make more angry than all but vulmea go on o'hara will dhrop an by the time the light 's light again there will be some six av we on the chest av mulvaney cryin murdher an rape mulvaney 's cot be near the ind door an the shmokin rifle will be lyin undher he whin
we have knock he over we know an all the rig'mint know that mulvaney have give o'hara more lip than any man av we will there be any doubt at the coort martial wud twelve honust sodger bhoy swear away the life av dear quiet swate timpere man such as be mulvaney wid his line av pipe clay roun his cot threatenin we wid murdher av we overshteppe ut as we can truthful testify mary mother av mercy think to mesilf it be this to have an unruly mimber an fiste fit to use
oh the sneakin hound the big dhrop run down my face for be wake wid the liquor an have not the full av my wit about lie shtill an hear thim workin themselves up to swear my life by tellin tale av ivry time have put my mark on wan or another an my faith they be few that be not so dishtinguishe twa all in the way av fair fight though for niver do raise my hand excipt whin they have provoke to ut tis
all well sez wan av thim

sez thry the kyard wid that he opine his tunic an tuk out the greasy palammer an they all fall in wid the notion deal on sez vulmea wid big rattlin oath an the black curse av shielygh come to the man that will not do his duty as the kyard say amin black jack be the masther sez kiss dealin black jack sorr shud expaytiate to you be the ace av shpade which from time immimorial have be intimately connect wid battle murdher an suddin death wanst kiss deal

thin they all cackle wid laughin duty thrippence sez wan av thim an damn cheap at that price but cud see they all dhrew little away from vulmea an lef he sittin playin wid the kyard vulmea sez
no word for whoile but lick his lip cat way thin he throw up his head an make the man swear by ivry oath know to stand by he not alone in the room but at the coort martial that be to set on he tould off five av the big to stretch on my cot whin the shot be fire an another man he tould off to put out the light an yet another to load my rifle he wud not do that himself an that be quare for twa but little thing considerin thin they swear over again that they wud not bethray wan another an crep out av the grass in diff'rint way two by two mercy ut be that they do not come on be sick wid fear in the pit av my stummick sick sick sick afther they be all go wint back to canteen an call for quart to put think in vulmea be there dhrinkin heavy an politeful to beyond reason fwhat will do fwhat will do think to mesilf whin vulmea wint away presintly the arm'rer sargint come in stiffin an crackin on not please wid any wan bekaze the martini henri bein new to the rig'mint in those day we use to play the mischief wid her arrangement twas long time before cud get out av the way av thryin to pull back the back sight an turnin she over afther firin'--as if she be snider fwhat tailor man do they give to work wid sez
the arm'rer sargint here be hogan his nose flat as table lie by for week an ivry comp'ny sendin their arrum in knock to small shivreen fwhat 's wrong wid hogan sargint sez i. wrong sez
the arm'rer sargint show he as though have be his mother the way av shtrippin tini an he shtrup her clane an easy tould he to put she to again an fire blank into the blow pit to show how the dirt hang on the groovin he do that but he do not put in the pin av the fallin'-block an av coorse whin he fire he be strook by the block jumpin clear well for he twa
but blank full charge wud ha cut his oi out look thrifle wise than boil sheep 's head how be that sargint sez i. this way ye blundherin man

he thank ye sargint sez will come to you again for further information ye will not sez he kape your clanin'-rod away from the breech pin or you will get into throuble wint outside an could ha dance wid delight for the grandeur av ut they will load my rifle good luck to thim whoile be away think and back wint to the canteen to give they their clear chanst the canteen be fillin wid man at the ind av the day make feign to be far go in dhrink an wan by wan all my roomful come in wid vulmea wint away walkin thick an heavy but not so thick an heavy that any wan cud ha tuk sure and thrue there be kyartridge go from my pouch an lyin snug in my rifle be hot wid rage against thim all an worry the bullet out wid my tooth as fast as cud the room bein empty then tuk my boot an the clanin'-rod and knock out the pin av the fallin'-block
oh twas music when that pin rowle on the flure put ut into my pouch an stick dab av dirt on the hole in the plate puttin the fallin'-block back that will do your business vulmea sez lyin easy on the cot come an sit on my chest the whole room
av you an will take you to my bosom for the big divil that iver cheat halter wud have no mercy on vulmea his oi or his life little care at dusk they come back the twelve av thim an
they have all be dhrinkin be shammin sleep on the cot wan man wint outside in the veranda whin he whishtle they begin to rage roun the room an carry on tremenju but niver want to hear man laugh as they do skylarkin too twa like mad jackal shtop that blast noise sez o'hara in the dark an pop go the room lamp cud hear o'hara runnin up an the rattlin av my rifle in the rack an the man breathin heavy as they stud roun my cot cud see o'hara in the light av the veranda lamp an thin hear the crack av my rifle she cry loud poor darlint bein mishandle next minut five man be houldin down go easy sez

she see one day--
ortheris say hastily for the romance of private ortheris be all too daring for publication look at the sun it be quarter past six lord three quarter of an hour for five an arf mile we will ave to run like jimmy o. the three musketeer clamber on to the bridge and depart hastily in the direction of the cantonment road when overtake they offer they two stirrup and tail which they accept enthusiastically ortheris hold the tail and in this manner we trot steadily through the shadow by an unfrequented road at the turn into the cantonment we hear carriage wheel it be the colonel 's barouche and in it sit the colonel 's wife and daughter catch suppress chuckle and my beast spring forward with light step the three musketeer have vanish into the night
and they be strong hand than mine that digge the ruby from the earth-- more cunning brain that make it worth the large desire of king and bold heart that through the brine go down the perfect pearl to bring lo have make in common clay rude figure of rough hew race for pearl strew not the market place in this my town of banishment where with the shift dust play and eat the bread of discontent yet be there life in that
oh thou who knowest turn and see as thou hast power over so have power over these because work they for thy sake and breathe in they mine agony small mirth be in the making now lift the cloth that cloke the clay and weary at thy foot lie my ware ere go forth to sell the long bazar will praise but thou-- heart of my heart have do well poor dear mamma the wild hawk to the wind sweep sky the deer to the wholesome wold and the heart of man to the heart of maid as it be in the day of old gypsy song scene.--_interior of miss minnie threegan 's bedroom at simla threegan in window seat turn over drawerful of thing
